text:
time bas surely come when personal feeling
end. the apimosities of the Press should become
forgotten. The absence of this unity is seri
on&x felt, and if this condition of circumstances
were changed, the Press of Jamaica would be
powerful, as it is in other communilies, for the
expression of publie end not private sentiment.
We have gone much ourselves out of the way
sometimes in our little differences with co-tem
poraries, and this, it is true, has not helped to
cement the harmony of newspajer life in Ja
maica, still there is a hope that as the island
gains in experience, a8 fresh enterprises creep
in upon ws, and as men generally benefit by
the steady progress of the Colony, a greater
harmony of feeling will no doubt prevail, and
, we certalnly shall not be the first to forget
that ¢ unity is strength,” or fail to recognize
the first glimpse of the olive branch of peace,
in the temper of any of our cotemporaries.
It is true, no two men can see and think alike,
but as Press crganizations live and prosper
and become powerful in other lands, tuere is no
reason why they should not he as prosperous,
as ueeful and as powerful in this country.
EFPECTS OF THE LATE HURRICANE.
The effects of the late hurricane are now
beginning to be seriously felt in the country
parishes, where fruit and ground previc ons
are grown ex’ -nsively. These ground: i il
be remembeicd, were torn up by tl;» Wi,
While huge rivers swept the grounds ot ne.
all that bad been planted. The consequence
is a serious scarcity, and a call from some
quarters to admit certain descriptions of bread
stuffs free of duty on the Nortbside. In Vere,
yams are being sold at 43d perlb, and -plap.
tains at an equally extortionate rate, while in
Lucea, Falmouth, and Montego Bay, yams
once worth 5%, and 63, per cwt., are now eager
ly bought up at 20s. per ewt. This condition
of eircumstances tells not only upon shvopkeep
ers and tradesmen, but upon everybody have
ing to purchase f,od and find the means of sub
sistence outside of their own premises The
Church collections have dwindled down in
almost every parish—the collections of the
Parochial Tax Collectors are below the ave
rage, and the Government very feelingly avoids
any pressure which would discourage the indus
trivus character of the peasantry in effurts to
surmount their agricultural difficulties.
THE COULIES.
Two Coolie vessels are expected from Cal.
outta with labourers for the KEstates; one of
these vessels will go to Annotto Bay or Port
Maria, the other to Westmoreland, to land. It
is reported by the ‘Falmouth Post’ thut “the
cause of the recent reported threatenings in St,
Thomas in the East was jealousy at the care
and attention bestowed bouth by the Govern
ment and the planters on the Coolie labourers
both when sick and well—but the native la
bourers have themselves to thank for this con
dition of affairs, for they will enter into no “in
dentures” for service, snd the capricious auod
utterly unreliable character of their service has
rendered the introduction of Coolie labour into
Jamaica adire necessity.”
THE LEGISLATION,
The recent Act throwing tke proof of honest
possession of Rum found in any person’s pos
session on the road, on the person so found, is
generally admitted fair, but the colynists fail
to see that such laws should pass for the pro
tection of the Government Revenue, and that
the same measure of protection from plunder
shonld be withheld from the proprietary or capi
talista clags, whose logwood, canes, yams and
plantains are carried off wholesale by their
own employecs, and there is no means of iden
tification, any more than for the rum this new
law has been passed to protect. Thes are
the Httlein'' nts of se'fish care whick | ' 1a
the industrii. (lasses because the Goy o
can ¢nact what laws are necessary for iis
_protectivn, but he same degree of negesse. :
protection for a hardworking body of commer
cial men is never thought of by our new !aw
makers. WHY sHouLp THIS BE? The thief
must show that he came by duty-paid Rum in
an hunest way-—but the same thief can steal
his master's logwood and dely the Judge and
the Poulice if his master cannot swear to the
identity of his.own wood.
CUBA.
The insurgents having succeeded in breaking
through the Spanish lines which confined them
to the Eastern province, are now firing Sugar
Plantations and committing other outrages,
ytain General Valmaseda has demanded a
begvy loan from the wealthy merchants of Ha
ﬁ_?. and they readily subscribed ii. Several
thousands of the Havana Vulunteers have
been summoned to hold themselves ready for
aotive service in the field.
.. THE BAHAMAS.
_ The Hotel is pretty well attended by Aweri.
visliors, but there are 60,000 visitors in
v lorida regretiing very much that there is no
g}’ o “Jfamaios open for the reception

goau [ ligine disanty it sh 49t v
s . et pilEgLh 00l i g
BT BRI E AV IS
prefer Jamaica to either Florida or Nas.
sau for a winter resort. Mr. Robertson, the
new Governor, is making himself extremely
popular.
HAXT].
The Spring rains have to all appearances set
in, and the immediate prospects of young cof
fee crop is wost hopeful. The French Steam
er “La Fontaine” has got ashore at Poit-au-
Prince, and is in a critical condition. Her car
go is damaged. An Euglish vessel-of-war is
endeavouriog to pull her off. The Legislative
Cbambers, summoned for April, will not pro
ceed to business before September. Several
persons have sought shelter in the Engiish and
American Consulates, fearing arrest at the
hand of the Government authorities.
PANAMA.
The Cable Steamers “In'~-national” and
“Dacia” has sailed for the Psciic, with a Sub
marine Cable which is to cc *hile, Peru,
and Panama immediately. .. . ..hew Grey,
the Manager of the India Ruhl - and Gutta
Percha Compsny of London, is to suysrintend the
cubmerging, and for this purp: - he left Eng
land on the 2nd April.
s U R ATF 3.
A Chinaman thus describes a trial ina U 8.
Court :—One man is silent, another talks all
the time, and twelve wise men condemn the
man who bas not spoken a word.
Reading.—Keep your view of men and
things extensive, and depend upon it that a
mixed knowledge is not a superficial ons, As
far as it goes, the views that it gives are true;
but he who reads deeply in one class of writers
only, gets views which are alwost sure to be
parverted, and which are not only narrow, but
false. Adjust your proposed amount of reading
to your time and inclination—that is perfectly
free to every man; but whether that amount
be large or small, let it be varied in its kind,
and widely varied. If I have a confident opin
fon on any one point conncoted with the im
provement of the human mind, it is on this.—~
Dr. Arnold.

text:
the information is ac¢curate, and that they
have been living in blessed ignorance of
what is going on around them. So much
for the Herald’s prestige and penetration
abroad.
The United States Steamer Getlysburg
has completed the soundings between St.
Jago de Cuba and Aunotto Bay (Ja.) for
the Cable which is to connect this Island
with the United States. Some soundingg
were 2% miles. She started for Aspinwall
on the Ist May on same mission.
TRINIDAD. .
- From the Port of Spain we have files
of papers to the 7th April.
I'he authorities were determined to
punish with severity, bands of women
who would persist in singing in the streets,
and in the most objectionable manner,
songs that were disgusting and obscene.
Eighteen of a band of young women
known as the Dahlias had been committed
for trial. .
The Chronicle is advocating railways in
preference to roads, and démands that
this has become an imperative and un
avoidable necessity in the country.
A fire in the Naparimas had consumed
nearly a whole district, originated from
the ashes of a lalggurer’s pipe.
Madame Cairoli, the celebrated voca
list, had arrived to give a series of con
certs.
The wharves are laden with sugar, but
there is no tonnage. 15,211 hhds., 1,735
tres., 1,050 brls. Sugar have been shipped
this year, with 4,001 phns., 125 tres. of
Molasses ; 44 puncheons Rum ; 4,242,680
Ibs. Cocoa.
The Duke of Buckingham has refused
to permit a bill to confine Lepers ina
hospital. . #®r. Bakewell with an experi
ence of a few months in the treatment of
lepers, concurs with his Grace.
ST. LUCIA. '
Our advices are from Castries to the
7th April. A Coolie was executed on
the 23rd for the murder of a Coolie wo
man. He exhibited such fear- on the
drop, he could hardly stand.
The St. Lucian is indignant at ritualis
tic Clergy in St. Vincent, refusing to re
cognise the Baptism of Wesleyans.
The rise in the Sugar market has sti
mulated the energy of the Planters.
. All the Coolie Immigrants have accept
ed S4O in lieu of return passage.
Noindentured Immigrants being on the
island in November, Immigration Agent’s
Office and services will be dispensed with.
: MARTINIQUE.
Advices of 18th March, reports illness
of Sir Arthur Rumbold, Governor of Tor
tola, just as he was about to sail for Eng
land. Fears were entertained of his life,
but through great medical care, and the
attention of the British Consul, Sir Arthur
has been pronounced out of danger. The
Antilles congratulates the Queen and the
people of England on the recovery of so
distinguished a man to give additional
brilliancy to England’ggreatness.
ST. VINCENT.
'The Episcopalians have been rebaptis
ing children already christened by Wes
leyans. The Archbishop of Canterbury
‘has decided that Baptism solemnized by
Non-Conformists is valid; and the Duke
of Buckingham, as Secretary of State for
the Colonies, sympathised with the of
fended Wesleyans, who appealed through
the Governor. The Church Clergy now
refuse to admit the authority of the Arch
‘bishop of Canterbury in St. Vincent, and
: ‘hééah op of Barbados sympathizes with
the C nii?; who has brought about“these
~ proceedings by his conduct.
SST.CROLX AVIS.
There have been several important
meetings of the Executive Council, buat
the proceedings have been sceret.
News from Kingstown is to band, bear
ing date 23rd April. :
The passengers on board the Dritish
Sloop Supertor, which was suppos=d to
have foundered off the Grenadines, about
eighteen months ago, are said to be alive
on the Spanish Coast, where the vessel
drifted, but that they have been enslaved.
The news reached Kingstown by a French
Steamer, on 22d April.
BARBADOS.
19,875 hogsheads sugar, and 7,361 pun
cheons molasses had been exported this
year from Barbados. A vessel with 50
head of Monte Video mules on board, had
arrived, but pat to sea again for another
port.
ANTIGUA.
Sugar making progresses briskly, and
notwithstanding some acceptable showers,
much more is wanted.
Sugar exported this year, 3,429 hhds,
455 tres., 1,474 brls.; and 1,698 phns.
Molasses.
ST. THOMAS.
Our advices are to the 2nd May. Mr.
Murphy, the diver, had completed his task
in fastening up the inlet valves of the
sunken Dock. She was two feet 1n the
mud. Efforts were to be made to pump
her out, and raise her on Monday the 4th.
Shareholders were sanguine of success.
Preparations were being made to pump
out the West India & Pacilic Steamship
“Columbian.”” Two powerful pumps
had been ordered from Eogland. Tue
rent in the ship’s side was being patehed.
The contractor is in a fairway to malke
a fortune.
The exportation to any of the Danish
Islands of bedding and goods damaged by
Seawater, 1s prohibited. :
Goveruor Birch announces that Barce
lona (Veneznela) is blockaded by the Gov
ernment of that Republic.
Cholera has completely disappeared.
There is a good demand for tonnage,
Fish of all deseriptions is enquired for.
Breadstufts and Provisions hight in stock.
Three U. S. Steamers *“De Soto,”
¢:Saco,” and “*Shawmot’’ are in port, to
protect the “Alice Ball” (ready for Sea)
from the Spanish War Steamer “Africa”
outside.
HAY'T'L
Our correspondence comes from Jacmel
to the 4th May.
A revolution has again burst over Hayti.
Salnave cannot escape from Qunaminthe.
General Chevalicr is missing. Salnave’s
ministers have been arrested ; others are
hiding in the Consulates.
The Cacos had reached Gonaives on
the North-west coast. At St. Marc (some
miles to the South, and in the same De
partment) General Nissage Saget had
issued a proclamation, calling the nation
to arms; it had been very generelly re
spond%l to. At Jacmel, on the South,
General Hecto, at the head of a large
army, supported by masses of the people,
drove the local authorities from their
offices at the point of the bayonet. He
was master of all the country there. At
Leogan, not many miles from Port-au-
Prince, General Faubert had appealed to
the people, and they also hoisted the flag
of revolution. . :
The Government troops were being
pressed on all sides.
‘The cruelties of Salnave, the treachery
of DeLorme, and a hatred of the policy of
Chevalier and Goarist Laroche, are all
subjects of animadversion in the political
harangues and revolutionary speeches.
Revolution everywhere!
~ MESQUITA COAST.
We have intelligence from Corp Island,
to the 9th April.
T'he Schooner Challenge arrived at Corn
' Island on the 28th February, with sixteen
: men on board, each with four loaded re
| volvers in his belt. She as strongly su
speeted of being engaged in the Slave
Trade. On hearing from Mr. Quin, the
! Senior Magistrate, that a British Steamer
' of-War was momently expected, the Chal
, lenge put mysteriously to sea and has not
| been heard of since.
| The U. S. Consul at Bluefields wrote
. immediately to New York requesting that
| a vessel of War should be sent without
| delay to Corn Island. :
Mr. Green the British Consul, had been
| apprized of the circumstances.
g THE BAHAMAS.
| The dis-establishment of **the Churches”
' in Nausau, is still the all-absorbing topic
‘ of interest. I'he House of Assembly will
| have no Established Church out of the
| taxes 0”10 Colony. 'The Legislative
; Counci™®ill not listen to the wholesale
| demolision whic® the Assembly desires,
| they accordingly refuse to pass the bill
| sent to them by the Assembly for thid® °
. purpose. The Assembly, maddened by
' the determination of the Council, has
called upon the Governor to disolve the
| Legislature, so as to ascertain the minds
. of the Colouists. The Governor knowing
. the voice of the people to be almost un
' animous in favor of the Assembly and the
| establishment of a perfect voluntary system
. throughout the Bahamas, refuses to listen
i to such a proposal. The House of As
| sembly, driven to desperation by these
| arbitrary proceedings, refuses to pass the
| ways aud means for 1868-1869—u “Dead
| Loek,” is the result, and the Dike of

ANOTHER REVOLUTION IN HAITI.
By the Packet tve have received late
and impor#ant intelligence from Haiti.
The standard of revolt has again been
raised. General Nissage Saget, resiging
at St. Mare, issued a proclamation, call
ing the nation to arms, as the depreda
tions and cruelties of which Salnave,
Delorme, and Evariste Laroche, had been
cuitty, were being carried too far.
This insurrectionary movement had
been responded to by the people of
Leogan, where General Petion Faubert
resides ; and in Jacmel, the most import
ant town of the South, where a force
had mustered having at its head General
Jean Pierre Hecetor, formerly Minister of
War of Geffrard.
T'he Cacos had joined the movement,
and were expected very shortly to make
their submission to the new Government.
A further s'atement is made that the
Ministers of Salnave had been arrested.
The revolution was spreading at Nip
pes, leremie, Miragoane, Anse-a-Veau,
Grand Goave, Petit Goave and Leogane.
The Cacos were pressing on the Gov
ernment troops from all directicns, and
it was believed that if Salnave had not
been taken prisoner, at least he must
have become so desperate of his position
as to have sought safety in flight. He and
others had taken refuge at the different
Consulates, and it is not unlikely that
they may arrive here in the Steamer

after it had been taken from a sidewalk. Then Professor McCafifrey kept
it in condition just for fun. It’s all a matter of maintaining the same
temperature.
Mr. Mount’s rabbit has the intelligence of a dog, actually hops into
church with its owner, sits beneath the pew. Its liking for crabmeat is
apparently due to eccentricity, wuich is something one might expect to

WHO'S NEWS
THIS WEEK...
By Lemuel F. Parton
NEW YORK.—Big, bulbous Gen.
Feng Yu-Hsiang craves action.
Among all China’s rampant war
lords, the old Christian general is
the least inclined
General Feng i, turn the other
Doesn’t Turn cheek, and the one
Other Cheek MO9St conspicuous
ly free from
charges of dealing under the table
with Japan. It was he, say the
news reports, who eased Chinese
troops into the Japanese Tientsin
concession, in civilian clothes, threw
Japanese strategy into confusion
and pretty nearly wrecked it. The
peasants love him, If ever a hu
man tidal wave.engulfs the invad
ing Japanese, he will be riding it.
He has been fighting, off and on,
for about 40 years, sometimes as a
regular and sometimes in more or
less private wars. The politicians
dislike him and every once in a
while have him sent off to the fog
belt. The last time was in 1929,
when he stirred up a revolt in an
effort to start an ‘“‘up-and-at-em’’-
movement against Japan. But they
always have to call him out of re
tirement, as when Chiang Kai-shek
was kidnaped last December.
Feng always has enough loyal sol
diers in reserve to count him in in
any large-scale ruction—he has
commanded as many as half 3 mil
lion men. When Chiang was res
cued, he made peace with Feng and
the latter assumed full responsibil
ity for the national military coun
cil, of which Chiang is chairman.
He was born of coolie parents in
Chaohsien. Anhwei. In June, 1900,
he was a big hulk
‘ing lad, stand
ing guard in a
Peking compound,
where some
@merican missionaries had been
trapped by the Boxers. He became
friends with Mary Morrill, a mis
sionary girl from Maine. One night
he had a terrible dream about a
snake under his bed. He asked Miss
Missionary
Unscrambles
Snake Dream
Morrill to interpret it.
What Miss Morrill made of the
dream is not recorded. A day or
two later, Feng saw a woman be
headed. He was troubled, without
knowing just why, and again saw
Miss Morrill. She converted him ta
Christianity. In the following years,
the fighting trade was brisk in Chi
na, and Feng began to get a repu
tation, now as a free lance and now
with the imperial armies. He
preached to his troops every day.
If his men didn't want to listen,
he used to pay them.
Each soldier wore a brassard,
pinned on with a safety pin, and
written on it the Ten Command
ments. But Feng had done a re
write job on the original. His ten
commandments gave specific direc
tions for overcoming an enemy in
different situations. Every so often,
Feng would announce that, no mat
ter what the exigencies of battle
might be, it was important for a
man to discover his own soul.
On these occasions, he would go
to the country, wearing an old pad-
ded coolie coat,
Stops B"“l? with a wvacuum
to Paint or bottle full of paint
brushes hung from
Read Poetry a cord around his
waist, He would paint plum blos
soms for a while and then squat on
his heels and read Confucius or
Lao Tze. After a few months of
this he would get back to his fight
ing.
When Chiang Kai-shek counted
him out in 1929, he went to a hovel
hung high on a rocky shoulder of
T’ai Shan, China’s ‘sacred moun
tain,” a place set aside for citizens
who wanted to take spiritual inven
tory. Nearby was the ancient ‘‘Hall
of the Five Sages.” .
Feng used the hall as a sort of
private university. He hired a fac
ulty of seven venerable teachers, he
being the only pupil. The curricu
lum listed first a course on ‘‘the
spring and autumn period of the
Chou dynasty.”
Feng is an ascetic, abstemious in
his diet, wearing cotton coolie
clothes, subjecting himself to rigid
discipline. In his retreat on the
sacred mountain, he rose every
morning at four o’clock.
His task was to draw on each of
four sheets of parchment the four
most beautiful
characters he
could possibly de
vise. His furniture
consisted of a
mat, a table and two straight
backed chairs and a tiny oil lamp.
He always went to bed at 8:30
o’'clock, to save oil, he said, and
to be rested for his early rising.
When Miss Morrill converted
him, she made him a member of the
‘“Way-Way-Whay,”” which means
“pbeautiful, beautiful society.’” It.is,
in essence, the Chinese name for
the Methodist church, but to Feng,
the frustrated artist, it was an or
ganization for the propagation of
beauty.
So when he isn’t fighting, he ful
fills his Christian duty, as he sees
it, by searching out beauty. He is
a pacifist and dislikes violence in
all forms, but before he gets back
to his plum blossoms and his Lao
Tze, he would like to get just one
good big walloping clout at Japan,

text:
6
A Reign of Wealth.
This nation was founded in weakness;
in has been prospered through habits of
simplicity; the individual’s industry and
its rewards have created wealth and in
dependence, and until recently the ave
' nues to high official positions have been
open to the ablest man, and his influence
there measured by his mind and not by
his money; and the plain, democratic,
independent statesman has been the
boast and pride of his constituency.
The severe simplicity of Jefferson’s
life has been the pride of democratic
Americans, though it was the object of
detestation by the aristocratic party
which he displaced. A president of the
United States, mounted upon his steed
and unattended, riding through the mud
of the infant capital to his inauguration,
savors of a grand independence, and
foretells an administration in which
brains shall rule. It is possible to yield
With becoming grace to physical or men
taLsuperiority, even though it be exer
cised on the side of injustice and wrong,
but men are always restive when under
the domination of money without
brains.
The seat of government at Washington
has become the resort of wealth and
splendor, display and dissipation, that is
making the winter sessions of Congress
a farce and a disgrace. Receptions and
entertainments, feasts and folly, have
shaped legislation mere than augment,
reason or logic. It is admitted that social
power, which means wealth and display,
is becoming supreme at the capital, and
that no man, unless he is able to in
fluence in his contemptible manner, has
a fair chance to rise by his merits.
Women lend the influence of their
charms and beauty to the base purpose
of political scheming, and a more effec
tive lobby than the old has been formed
in the mansion and the banquet hall.
Writes the Washington correspondent
of the Boston Advertiser:
"A mail’s wife really counts for more
in Washington than he. Give him
money enough to keep up a handsome
establishment and a wife who knows
how to entertain, and his position here
is secure. ’ That is really what accounts
for the rows of elegant houses which are
filling up the late pastures of the West
End. The men who want to lead here
have found that the surest path to their
goal is througtPthe portals ot a mansion.
* * * A bureau officer wants to get
into the cabinet, and he gives little din
ners; a senator aspires to be made an
ambassador, and his wife increases greatly
the scope ol her receptions ; a gentleman
has a son whose promotion in the navy
is slow, and he attends assidiously to
haunting official receptions; young men
understanding the true inwardness of
affairs who are after something which
affords the minimum of work and the
maximum of pay find it to their advan
tage to do society. * * * And yet I
think that the wholesale criticism of
\Vashington women is unjust. They are
in an atmosphere which is redolent of
politics, Jts nrizes are the brightest
hued fruit on the tree, and what wonder
that they early learn to be adepts in
wiles in behalf of their husbands and
sons. Is it strange that they smile upon
Senators, who may, perhaps, some day
have to decide upon momentous quest
ions to them? It seems-not.”
Are such tendencies healthful ? Ace
they not rather subversive of the princi
ples of simplicity and mental supremacy
by which we have become strong?
It is estimated the present senate rep
resents $600,000,000. It is fast becoming
a patrician body, and recent elections of
senators have shown that the people’s
power has only been able to displace one
millionaire by substituting another. Let
the people everywhere resolve to elect
to stay at home every man who attempts
to make a splendid spectacle, or fails to
make his influence felt by sheer, force of
brains.—Cape Ann Bulletin.
Educate ! Agitate ! Organize !
The attention of our readers is called
to the advertisement of Dr. Dameron.
The doctor’s neat dental parlors are in
the Union block, Sixteenth and Law
rence, where he and his attentive assist
ants are always ready to attend to the
wants of their customers. Those in want
of superior dental work we advise to call
on Dr. Dameron.
j —■ ,
i Educate! Agitate! Organize!
THE NEEDFUL.
There are' a great many who have sub
scribed for The Enquirer and have not
yet paid us anything. We need money
to keep the paper going, and if those of
our friends indebted to us will settle, it
will be a great accommodation right at
present. We do not want anyone to stop
reading the paper, but those who can
spare it will confer a favor by paying as
soon as convenient. Bring or send on
your mites, and keep your paper boom
ing. Every little helps, you know.
Let Your Light Shine.
Dr. Wagner, the celebrated specialist
of Denver, Colorado, 343 Larimer street
believes in letting the world know what
he can do, and is doing for thousands of
his fellowmen. His treatment for lost
manhood is sure to win him a name that
posterity will bless. Ten thousand tes
timonials from all over the United States
from those he has cured is proof positive
that he does cure the worst cases of
these diseases. The afflicted from
chronic and sexual diseases of every
kind will find him their best friend.
Read his advertisement in all our city
papers and call on him for advice, as we
know you will corroborate us in saying
he is the sufferer’s true friend.
Job Work.
We are prepared to do all kinds of job
work in the highest style of the art, at
reasonable figures. Understand, any
kind of job work. If you are needing
anything, call on us, 370 Larimer street,
over C. Frick’s shoe store.
The Chinese.
The Daily Report truly says: “The
legislature of Nevada has bad presented
to Congress a formal protest against the
nullifying of the Anti-Coolie restriction
law by the secretary of the treasury,who
has usurped powers greater than those
possessed by the President. The legis
lature of California should have taken
similar action. The secretary of the
treasury has simply acted in behalf of
certain corporations that have made
money out of coolie immigration and
coolie labor. Contempt for law is a cor
poration-characteristic which the masses
of the American people may yet acquire,
and apply to the cases of those corpora
tions. What the Pacific coast requires
is a stringent law prohibiting Mongolian
immigration forever, under penalty of
death.”
Educate! Agitate ! Organize!
The Dangers of Excess.
The married man who thinks he can
commit no excess, because he is married
will suffer as certainly as the debauchee.
Till he is told, the idea never enters his
head that he has been guilty of almost
criminal excess; nor is this to be won
dered at, as such a case of disease is not
even hinted at by the family physician.
Over excitation, irritability, inflamation,
dyspepsia, palpitation of the heart, de
pressed spirits, loss of genetic power,
sleep is not refreshing, and a jaded lan
guid Indifference takes the place of
energy and ambition. A vast amount of
ill-health arises from this unsuspected
cause and thousands go to premature
graves that might be restored to health
by using Dr, Wagner’s treatment for
lost manhood. See his ad on another
page.

COOLIE LANTERN
BURNS PLANE
PEIPING (I.N.S.).—'The thought
lessness of a Chinese coolie tak
ing a lighted lantern into the
cabin of an American “Air Yacht’’
cost the China National Aviation
Company the loss of the $120,000
amphibian.
Fortunately, the American pilot,
George (“Irish”) Hamil, who was
supervising the covering of the
plane for the night, escaped in
jury when an explosion was fol
lowed by flames, leaving nothing
but a twisted mass floating on
the water at Hangchow.
The Chungking, as the amphib
ian was named, had been flown to
Hangchow a few days before and
Hamil was standing on one of the
wings when the coolie made his
fatal visit into the cabin. The
latter escaped with severe bums.

Nathan Warfield, who has grown
to a weight of 230 pounds in
the years he has been on desk
duty at police headquarters, in
formed the town council’s police
committee that, orders or no or
ders, he couldn’t ride a motor
cycle- and he wouldn’t try. The
committee had him suspended
forthwith. Warfield said:
“I can’t learn to ride one
of those things in front of
the gang of unemployed who
wait in front of the city hall
in the hope of seeing me fall
off.”
The council is wholly Demo
cratic. The police force is all
The council prompt
ly ordered the police to resign.

THE CRY OF THE HEART.
"I want some one to play with me,"
A little toddler cries,
As he looks for a wee Joy-sharer,
With tears in the clouded eyes.
"I want some one to love me,
Some one to tell me so."
Be it heart of youth or maiden,
The wish is whispered low.
"I want some one to talk with me ;
I am weary of being alone,"
Is the silent eohe in grandma's heart ;
For love she makes her moan.
i MUTINY ON A COOLIE SHIP.
i4 One of the Tragedies of a Branch
of the Slave Trade.
.a
When slavery was abolished in
the Brazils there. sprang up to take
its place a traffic hardly less accursed,
the coolie trade. The coolie was a
Chinaman who agreed. to leave his
sountry for a term of years at stated
wages, but after a while vessels were
fitted out and dispatched to various
islands in the Pacific and other seas,
and thousands of natives were ab
deuted and sold into perpetual slav
ery. These abducted people also
esame to be called coolies. Soon after
the trade opened the Chinese govern
cment, which received so much for
every coolie shipped away, began send
ing off its offenders and malefactors
along with honest laborers. Instead
of giving a thief the bastinado he was
sent to the coolie barracks; instead of
lopping off the head of a pirate he was
deported. Among every 500 coolies
were at least 100 m-lefact3rs, and re
volts at sea were of frequent occur
rence. In the year 1858 seventeen
vessels in the coolie trade were either
burned at sea or captured by the na
tives aboard, and in case of capture
not a man was spared.
Our ship, the Princess, of New
York, was lying at Macao, south of
Hong-Kong, in the fa'l of 1868, when
four different craft were loading with
coolies at the same time. Macao was
one of the great shipping points, and
everything was carried on under the
supervision of government officials. I
saw a Brazilian brig take on 4891
coolies, and a worse lot of men could
not possibly have been scraped to
gether in any country. They ware to t
be housed between decks, a:(l the brig J
carried two cannon and ten soldiers to I
keep them in sub;ection. They must a
be looked after as sharply as convicts a
on their way to Australia in the old c
days of the penal settlements. This t
brig, which was called the Don, got f,
away a week ahead of us, and,through t
some blunder, with only a few of her a
water-casks filled. She had head a
winds for five days, and we finally b
overtook her before she had made 300
miles on her long voyage. We sighted 14
her as the wind was dying out one af
ternoox,, and were carried down to
within a mile of her before we lost
steerageway. We could make out her
name with the glass, but even without
that the odor which caule across the
water to our nostrils would have proved *
her a coolie ship. As our captain ob- h
served, after shuttingp his glass and
snifing the air:
vL "That smell would settle it if I
was deaf, dumb and blind and had no
nose; she's a coolie!"
e It was about six o'clock, when we
it lost the breeze, and the crew knocked
y off for supper and spent the following
, three hours yarning and smoking.
d The brig was all right, so far as we
could observe, and we had no partic
ular interest in watching her. About
5 nine o'clock, however, .we were all
r. startled into sudden activity by a
d most horrible din from the coolie. It
seemed as if' two or three hundred
Svoices were yelling in chorun, and the
7 sound did not cease until broken in
s upon by the 'reports of frearms.
S "I'll tell you exactly what's taking
place on board that fellow," said the
mate, who had been listening itently.
"He's short of water,and the wretches
Shae been put upon half or quarter
b allowance. They are suffering- and
desperate, sad the crew have had to
shoot down, some of the leaders.
'rve seen 200 likely heathens go dead
for the want of water, but they didn't
die without making a tremendous ef
s fort to take the ship. We'll hear from
that chap againinside of an hour, and
I'll miss my plum duff if he isn't
alongside of us before long bartering
for fresh water."

bell ano hour there was another shorus
of yels, shrieks and ecur~s, haid we

"Come,partner.let us have our walk,"
Says an old man, bent and tried
To the faithful dog,whose love he knows
Will never be denied.
The world is full'of children,
From one to eighty-one,
Crying for loving comradeship,
The one thing neath the sun
That satisfies the spirit,
And makes all else seem tame ;
Be It health, or wealth, or knowledge,
Be it beauty, or wit, or fame.
-Ellen Fraeker Pratt, In Washington Star.
in hour before was repeated, only there`
ce was more savageness to it this time.
i, It did not die away as before, but kept
a increasing in intensity and lasting so
is long that the mate leaped upon the
id rail and looked toward the brig and
-a muttered:
Is "The scoundrels are making a
a, break at last! Why don't the men
u- se the guns?"
r- His words were followed by the
'0 reports of a dozen muskets, and they
ir were still cracking away when some
- one stood on the rail of the brig and
r waved his hat in our direction.
* "The coolies are loose!" exclaimed
e the mate as he made for his boat.
d "That signal is for me, but I'll return
S for the water as soon as the outbreak
f is put down."
a He was of in a moment, and his
s men pullei such a stroke that their
boat was carried to the brig's side in
four or five minutes. She was run
I under the bows" and the men scram.
r bled up over the chains. We knew
that the outbreak was serious, for the
Shubbub increased, and we could see
the crew rushing wildly about. There
was a continued pop of musketry, and
f we became so excitei that every man
I in the ship, cook included, hurried
i .oft to look down upon the stranger's
decks. As we lay broadside to each
other, and the distance was so short,
we could see all that took place. De
spite the desperate efforts of the crew,
tVe coolies were finding their way on
deck. They were shot and clubbed
and hacked, but they came faster than
they could .be taken care of, and in
half an hour from the time the mate's
boat left us, the Chinese had posses
sion of at least one-half the brig. They
got hold of some of the*muskets and
cutlasses, and they knew how to use
the former as well as the sailors. The
forward gratings were pried off and
the hold emptied of its living cargo,
and during all this time there was
fierce fighting in the waist of the
brig.
The coolies had no fear, and once
loose their savage natures were thor
oughly aroused. When they got pos
session of half the vessel they cap
tured the water-butt on deck, and we
saw them drinking with the bullets
fying past their ears. The water
seemed to add to their desperation,for
soon after getting possession of it a
hundred or more made a grand rush,
armed with every sort of weapon, and
after a conflict of five minutes they
had possession of the ship. Three t
of the crew saved their lives for the I
time being by running aloft, but all
the others seemed to have been killed.
You cannot suppose that we were
quiet spectators of such a scene. We
wanted to be called away to help sup
press the outbreak, but our captain
had a mortal hatred for coolies and
announced that he would not interfere
in the slightest. It cut us med,
though,to see the crew making a fight
.against such odds, and gradually fall
ing back and fighting to the last, with- d
out getting our help. When the grand
rush was made, and the remnant of
the crew driven aloft, a sort of groan
went through our ship, and more than
one man muttered against our cap
tain's policy. When the Chinese got t
full possession many of them drank
sea water, and it was, only a short r'
time before a eask of rum wasbroached
sad served out to all. When the vile a
stuff began to take effect fury was let 0
loose aboard the brig. B-Seh' surging
to and frol Such jabbering and shout- c
ing and screaming! The bodies of the 0
dead sailors were stripped naked,
'icked about the decks and mutilated a
in the most horrible manner,and then gi
flung overboard. At- the very first i
splash we saw at least a dosen sharks
the spot, and after that their n
Sor were cutting the water in i
::very rtio ..
As Xld you before, three of the
brig's ew escaped instant death by
rantnag where they were for the
time t tten. They went as high as
they/..uldgo and concealed themselves
as . l l possible. They dared not
shout to ts, but they implored us by
getures to come to their relief, and
gapve:s to understand that the coolies
aspposed our bark to be loaded with
Chinese als, .and that we would be
attaked in an effort to set them free.
hey also -made it clear, the night
belag almastas light as day, that the
fellows had a score of muskets, and
we. r ould easily understand that a
sharip cutass in theha nds of a des
perate goolie would be a wicked

sea. The idea was appalling, not only
on account of the great height, but
those wicked dorsal fins were cutting
ra the water about the brig in a way to
make us shiver. The moment finally
came when the first sailor dropped
from the end of a yard, and he was
speedily followed by his companions.
Each struck the water with an awful
splash, went out of sight, and nothing
further was seen of them. If they
were stunned by the fall so much the
better.
It was about 11 o'clock when the
coolies made ready to attack us. The
brig had three boats which they could
get at, and each boat was crowded
with men. They had muskets, cut
lasses, capstan-bars, belaying pins
and other weapons, and while they
were ma-ing preparations they kept
up a yelling and whooping which
would have done credit to Indians.
Those who were to remain aboard
defied and taunted us and evidently
considered us certain victims. I don't
think any man of us felt what people
call fear as we saw the thres boats
leave the brig and pull toward us, but
we could be forgiven for doubting
whether we had strength to beat them
off. The boats kept together, and it
was evident that they meant to board
on the same side. This was the better
for us. Our bricks and other weapons
were hastily moved, and while we were
at this work the captain stood on the
rail and shouted to the coolies and
warned them tokeep off. The answer
to this was a shot from a musket, and
the ballet whistled so close to his
head that he lost no time in getting
off the rail. We saw that we had got
to fight, and we were as ready as we
could be when they laid us aboard.
Fortunately for us they had no strat
egy about them, but the three boats
pulled together for our bows and
hooked on in a crowd. The bark had
very high bulwarks, and the Chinese,
even with their muskets, could do us'
no damage. We had but one point to
defend, and a score of brickbats and a
couple of pails of hot water took the
sand out of the crowd pretty quick.
In three minutes after getting under
our bows they realized that they had
out out a larger job than they could
manage, and they were a well-scalded
set as they let go and pulled to the
brig.
Three-fifths of the crowd had been
left on board, and the failure of the
attack was greeted with such yells as
mortal man never heard before. The
boats were not hauled up at all, and
the beaten coolies were no sooner
aboard the brig than a terrific fight
took place, which did not stop until 25
or 30 had been knocked on the head
and flung over to the sharks. About
midnight the boats were filled with
men to attack us again, but there was
a great jabbering and gesticulating,
and the plan was finally abandoned.
At 1 o'clock the calm was broken,
though the breeze which reached us
was a light one. The captain weighed
all the circumstances in his mind, no
doubt, and he came to the conclusion
that it was the best way for us to con
tinue our voyage and hope to fall in
with a cruiser. The coolies set up a
howl of vengeance as we made sail,
and that we could not have made
friends with them was proved by the
fire of musketry which they kept up
until we were out of hearing. The
brig was turning round and round
on her keel when last we saw her,and
some of the fellows were aloft trying
to loosen the sails. Thirty hours
later we met IH, If. S. Defiance, and t
gave her captain the brig's bearings,
but it was a full year before I learned ,
any further particulars. After a cruise
of a week the man-of-war found the
eraft bottom up at sea, a long way
from where we left her, with never a
sign of a human being, dead or alive,
in her vicinity. She had probably
been caught in a squall, and as the
coolies knew nothing of seamanship
the brig had turned turtle and
drowned them to the last man.
New York Sun.
How the Hlorse Died.
Having reached the firing-line,
many officers left their horses tied to
the brush on the sands of San Juan
river. Baggage and gun mules were
turned loose, and stood stupidly
about. There was a constant tweet
of bullets coming through the trees
from the spanish position. One horse
caught three almost in a bunch; an
other passed through them, and he
lay down on his side, panting desper
ately. A big gun-mule lay on his side
gasping, and another horse sat down
like a dog, giving every evidence of
great pain., A ball out the skin on a
mule's knee, but he only stamped the
leg as though to get rid o6 a trouble
some fy.
The thing about it which was
strange to me was that the horses

Why do not horses die for their

why speak- of these things? It is saf

- - "COOLIES.
A Species of 'Slavery Existing in a
Brilisli Colony.
Notwithstanding British criticism of the
manner in which
South' arc treated
the colored people of the
by 4 lie whites, there arc
aonic things about the treatment of the free
labor problem in the British island colony
of Trinidad that sccni curiously mediaival in
principle.
Soon after the negroes of Trinidad were
freed, now more than GO years ago, the
plan tore, upon the plea, doubtless just
unougn, that the freedmen would not work,
obtained from the Government special legisla
tion providing for the importation of ISast
Indian coolie laborers. "WJieu tho importa
tions of coolies began negroes were demand
ing ixoni GO cents to $1.25 a day, according
A Hindoo Piuest.
to the work they were employed to do. The
law authorising the importation of coolies
provided tlmt they should contract to remain
with their employers for a term of years,
tvorking for 25 cents a day, and that they
should meanwhile have medical attendance
and be returned free of charge to their
homes. A special export tax on. the pro
ducts of the plantations was laid to pay
ibr the transportation of the coolies between
India and Trinidad. The-importation of the
coolies has been going on ever since that
time, until now from that source and from
natural increase there are GO, 000 coolies in a
population of 200,000. The white population
is small, so that by far the larger part of the
inhabitants are negroes and coolies. The
former, in competition with contract labor at
25 cents a day, cam from 45 to GO cents a
day. Kegro mechanics earn as high as SI a
day. The negroes are a stronger race than
the coolies, and the heavier work still falls
upon them. They are the mechanics of the
island, and there are many negro profession
id men. Low as. is the htandard of wages of
the Trinidad mechanics, their labor is really
not cheap io the employer according to stand
ards in the United States. A bricklayer in
Trinidad, for example, working upon a plain
12-iuch wall, will lay from 200 to 300 bricks'
a day, while a-bricklayer in the United States
doing similar work will lay from 1,000 to
1,500.
Trinidad is a Crown colony, and the plant
ers are powerful in Governmental affairs.
At their demand coolie importations continue,
though there is considerable opposition to the
system, in view of the fact that wages are
kept low and industrial depression has made
necessary the raising of funds in aid of the
unemployed.- There was a strong protest
from Trinidad when it. was proposed to send
thither from the densely populated island of
Barbadoes, wheie there are 1,100 inhabitants
to the square mile, the surplus negro labor,
and to provide the negiocs thus imported
with land to be cultivated as their own.
There had never been any such act of charity
in aid of the Trinidad negroes squeezed by
comiKJtition with contract coolie labor.
There were imported into Trinidad in the
10 years from 164 to 3WJ3 more than 24,000
East Indian coolies, and it is believed that
the miiiortations of last year exceeded 2,000.
It is i barged that the coolies contribute very
largely to the criminal population of the
inland, and statistics show that while the
Xast Indians constitute 32 -per cent, of the
population, they contribute 4G per cetlt. of
the convicts.
The coolies come under a contract to stay
10 years on the island, five years in a species
of slavery at zio cents a da, the other five as

tract the coolie is liable to arrest and seven

days' imprisonment if found ff his planta
tion without a permit from his master. It is
charged, though not publicly and in print,
that the coolie women, though under contract
at 25 cents a day, often receive considerably
lower wages, and coolie children do light
-work at much smaller pay. Some of the

Strang, but True
coolies are old and decrepit, though tho im
portations are mostly of men and women in
the priino of life. With the laborers come
their priests, Hindoo and Mahometan.
The Hindoos, by the way, greatly exceed in
number the Mahometans. When tho first
live years of the contract expire some of the
coolies establish themselves as fruit growers
or small shopkeepers, or in other lines of in
dustry. The coolie, like the negro, lives in a hut of
bamboo, plastered on the outside. The huts
built by the planters are often roofed with
corrugated iron, while those built by tho
laborers themselves usually have palm-leaf
roofs. A single plantation sometimes has
several hundred coolies. .They live with the
utmost frugality. They are bred from in
fancy in their native country to a very light
fare, and the climate of Trinidad is such that
clothing and shelter arc of the lightest kind.
Probably no laborer of the United States, not
even the Southern plantation negro, lives so
cheaply. The men arc clad at ordinary
limes mainly in the cummerbund, which
varies in length from two to eight yards, and J
is wound about tho middle for a brccchelout.
The longest cummerbunds are sometimes
wound down tho legs well toward the knees
so as to form a sort of trousers. IVhcu the
men visit Port of Spain, the capital, they
usually wear a short white cotton jacket,
The white turban is their headjrear. The I
women are very lightly clad. Their gula
attire consists of graceful draperies of light
stuff, including a sort of hcaddrc&s falling to
the forehead, but not altogether concealing
the smooth, straight, glossy black hair.
Some of the women have a smooth, brown
beauty that is extremely attractive. Tho
skin suggests carefully-browned coffee, and
one realizes that there are other beautiful
complexions than those of tho white races.
Doth mon and women have fine, regular
"features, and well-made heads. The lithe,
eiect body of the coolie, with nothing but tho
whiteuessof the turban and fho'cummcrbnnd
to reliovc its smooth brown, is in flue con
trast with the sloiichiness of the negro labor
ers. It is to the thrift of the coolie that we owe
the familiar though curious silver ornaments
that the women wear, and that travelers fetch
houiefiom Trinidad and other parts of the
West Indies, where these people live. The
coolie turns his savings into silver ornaments,
beating the coins into bracelets, armlets,
neck-rings, uose-riugs, and the like. There
is great variety in these things, though the
general character of the Oriental decoration
is preserved in all. Sometimes a neck-ring
is merely a large hoop, with coins pendant
from its whole- periphery. Sometimes there
is a double row of coins strung upon a cord
and hung about the neck so as to fall nearly
to the waist. Often several of these rings of
different sizes are put about the neck of a
woman. A man's riches arc known by the
sjilendor of the adornments worn by his wife.
Some of the women have their arms covered
""i.'.r'Cl
Wives of Kicn Coolies.
with silver ornaments of various shapes and
sizes. Those worn about the elbow are often
broad pliant bands of picked work held to
gether at the ends with gay Btrings of many
strands. The wrists are adorned with lialf a
dozen silver bracelets of different weights,
put on by bending them so that they will
slip over thehaud. Some of these are wrought
with much labor, though the decoration is
usually coarse when examined at close
hand. There is said to be a peculiar and
proper order in which a bet of bracelets should
be worn. Many women wear a little Toset of
silver at the side of the no, and others a
huge nose-ring. The ueekrings are heavy
and valuable. Doubtless the coolie has
suffered by the fall in silver, but he still pins
his faith to it with a blind zeal that would
put to shame the modest advocate of free sil
ver coinage at a ratio of 1G to 1.
American visitors to the West Indies have
long been familiar with the manner in whyth
the coolie wife sells her ornaments to the
buyer willing to pay the price fixed by the
husband. The women stick to that price in a
way that speaks volumes for marital
" UIUI, bJKSIKb YOIUIUCB HUT Uianiai .lUlllOr-
W - Every visitor with a little loose coin to
1CT.11
spare brings home some trophies from the
anus of the coolie women, and the more com
mon forms of coolie ornaments have become
familiar to many poisons in the United States.
As the coolie wealth is placed in silverorna
racnts, such things arc usual objects of theft
among these people, and many coolies arc
arraigned on the charge of stealing neck-rings,
armlets, and bracelets. Theft is a common
crime among the coolies, ad the temptation
to commit it is strong because of the open
character of the houses occupied by these peo
ple. New York Sun.

A VERY OLD GAME.
Golf Sufi Been Known to a, Great 3Iany
Peoples..
Scribncr's Jfagasinc
The original of the royal and ancient game
of golf is lost in obscurity. "Whether it was
an evolution from the kindred games of kolf,
hockey, or jeu de mail, whether developed iti
Scotland or carried thither from Holland,
may never be definitely ascertained.
Its record is woven into Scotch history,
legislation, and literature from the ljcgiuning
of recorded time. More than 400 years ago
it was a popular game in Scotland, and
archery, the necessary training for a soldier,
bo lunguished in competition with it that, by
the stem ordinance of Parliament and royal
decree it was proclaimed that " the fut ball
and golf be utterly cryit donn and nocht
usit."
But although forbidden to the people, it
was a favorite ro3'al pastime. King James
played it with Bothwell in 1353, and the
royal accounts show that he had money on
the game ; Queen Mary played it after the
death of Darnley, peihaps as a solace in her
widowhood; JumcsVI., an early protection
ist, laid a heavy tariff on golf balls from Hol
land, and gave a monopoly of ball making at
four shillings each balL to a favorite. The
great Marquis of Montrose played at St. .An
drews and Ieith Links, and was lavish in his
expenditure for golf balls, clubs, and caddies.
The news of the Irish rebellion came to
Charles I. while playing a match at Leith.
James II., when Duke of York, won a four
some, with an Edinburg shoemaker as a
partner, against two Englishmen. The shoe
maker built a house fn the Cauongate with his
share of the stakes, and, in order to com
memorate the origin of his fortunes, placed
on it& walls as escutcheon a hand dexter grasp
ing a club, with the motto, "Far aud Sure."
John l'orteous, of the u Hoartof Midlothian,"
Duncan Forbes, of Cullodcn, who turned the
tide of Prince Charles's fortunes in 1745,
were adepts at the game, and Covenanters in
their sermons, poets, philosophers and novel
ists have paid their tribute to the royal sport

MONUNENT.
Slassivo Granite Shaft Dedicated
to tho
Memory of Rebel Soldiers.
Tho Confoderato monument was dodicntcd
nt Chicago Memorial Day, May 30. Tho weather
wns pleasant and the nttondanco very largo.
Tho officers assembled at tho Palmer llouso
early in tho day.
About tho corridors could bo seen army offi
cers in full uniform; Grand Army mon wear
ing tho fatigue uniform of 16G1-G5; members
of the militia of several States in gorgeous ar
ray, and eX'Con federates with the badges of
tho various Calnps to which they belonged.
Among tho throng of prominent peoplo were
Lougstreet, Fitzhugb Lee, John C. Black, John
M. Palmer, Marcus J. Wright, Joseph Stockton,
M. E. Butler, Eppa Hunton, John C Under
wood, projector of tho raonumcut, and Gen.
Wntle Hampton, orator of tho day.
Besides these thoro woro hundreds of other
oflicera of both sides who went to honor tho
memory of tho men who lio buried around and
beneath tho massivo granite shaft orcctotl by
their comrndes-in-arms on tho beautiful shores
of Lake Michigan. Tho Chicago City Troop,
under command of Cnpt. Funkhauser, acted .13
escort. Othororganizations which took part iu
tho parado were: Battery D, Illinois National
Guard; tbo Black Hussars, Capt. T. S. Quincy
commauding,.and detachments from the Illinois
militia. Grand Army Posts and Camps of Con
federate veterans made up tho body of tho pro
cession. The exorcises began shortly after 11 o'clock.
Col. Joseph Desha Pickett, Chaplain f tho
Kentucky "Orphan Brigade," invoked tho
divine blessing. Gen. John C. Underwood, who
originated the idea of tho monument, then
briefly skotclicd the incoption and progress of
tho monument, which culminated in the nroat
gatherjug, and closed by introducing as tho
presiding officer of tbo exorcises, Bcv. H. W.
Bolton, pastor of Contcnnry Methodist Church,
and Past Commander of U. S. Grant Post, 28,
G.A.B., whom ho oulogized as tho 'embodi
ment of honor, justico nud morality, o broad
representative of tbo Union veteran, a humani
tarian and a Christian gontlomau."
Dr. Boiton then delivered an culoev on tho
veterans of the lato war, both North and South.
He wns followed by Gen. Wado Hampton, who
was orator of tho day, and made a speech
worthy of tbo occasion. After Gen. Hampton's
address canio tho impressive ceremony of
monumenting the guns. Thoro wero four
of those cannon, all captured from tho Union
forces at Murfrepsboro, and afterwards used by
the Confederates in a number of important
battles, among which wero those of Missionary
Itidgo, Kesaca, Dalton, Kcncsaw Mountain,
Peach Tree Creek, Atlanta, and Fruklin. These
guns wero retaken by tho Uuiou forces at
Nashville.
Tho composition of tho monumenting corp3
was:
For gun No. 1. Col. B. II. Stewart and Miss
Lucy Hill.
For gun No. 2. Col. S. J. Sullivan and Mrs.
Albert Akere.
For euu No. 3. Col. Goo. Forrestor and Mis3
Laura M. Mitchell.
For gun No. 4. Col. John W. White and Miss
Bollo Armstrong.
Spiking party. Col. B. Leo Franco and Cora
radoTlicodoreNc.il. TJio monumenting corps and tbo spiking
party formed in lino, tho gcnllemon escorting
tho ladies, and passed to their respective sta
tions through double lines of Union aud Con
fcdciato veterans, who stood uncovered.
The ceremony which attended the "monu
menting" of gun No. 1 was practically tho
same for all the cannon, and was as follows:
Col. Stewart: "This gun, having fired its
last shot, will now bo silenced forovor. Spiko
thoguu." Whereupon tho spiking party spiked
tho cannon. Col. Franco placing the spiko, and
Colorado Noal driving it home.
Miss Hill then mounted tho pedestal and
said : " This cannon, with its glorious record on
the field of battle, having been silenced for
ever, I do consecrate it to the memory of tho
valorous soldiery we now monument, as a mil
itary decoration for their bravery and honor
until death."
The guu3 having boon monnmented, tho
drums gaved a muffled roll, and tho'entiro

The latest news from Hawaii if in
further couflfinalion of Tho Calls
contention Wo quoted President
tolo aud other Island authorities
buforeanncxatioito provo that t hoy
expected pormission to continue
coolie contract labor there and to

This was hotly denied bv annexa

suraneo that our labor laws would
bo i xtiitided to tho islands and that

pouk is of tho American taxpayers

At a rocrut meeting of the Dole

action upon these petitions brought

iMauds ht labor under the rotn

This admitsion that abuses esist
aud that there is inrsonal bodily
ill treatment uf thesa uuforiunato
and servile people marks iu a start

Hawaiian labor tystem aud ours
Yet the two countries are under tho

diction
The people of Hawaii aro to bo
congratulated Their public expen
ses are paid by ue Their leading

It is true that there rises bpyond

nexation organs woro presenting tho

Herald Cameramen Tell Their Story
AT AN HISTORIC HOME '-Beauvoir," historic home of Jefferson Davis, president of the Confed
eracy, at Biloxi, Miss., is the scene of old-fashioned dances by aged Confederate veterans and their
wives in preparation for the fortieth Confederate reunion at Biloxi.
AUTO FUNERAL PYRE An
automobile safety committee of
Long Beach, Cal., begins to rid
highways of unsafe cars by heap
ing 200 cars into one pile and..
igniting them.
XA:-::xwa4::::.:':-:'::'X:
NEWEST KIND OF TRANSCONTINENTAL FLIGHT An "air train" across the continent from San
Diego, Cal., to New York is the newest stunt J. D. ("Duke") Jernigin, Jr., left, inset, towing with a
powerful airplane a glider piloted by Captain Frank Hawks, right, inset, holder of both cast and west
transcontinental flight records.
QUEEN GUARDED Mias Al
laire Fleming, of Las Cruces, N.
M., queen of the Mecca ball, an
nual 'celebration of the engineer
ing college of the University of
Iowa, had to be guarded against
kidnaping by law students in a
traditional feud.
POOR MAN'S CAR This car, appearing in Europe, requires neither license nor garage. It is propelled
in the same manner as an ordinary cycle. Chassis and complete car are shown here.
TRIES TO SELL STOCK IN HIS
FUTURE William J. Nolan, for
merly a milkman, says he asked
wealthy Chicago women to buy
shares in his future, promising to
give notes with interest and mean
while be at their command should
they need his services.
T
NEW LORD BALFOUR The
Hon. Gerald W. Balfour, above,
succeeds his brother, the late Earl
of Balfour, last of Victorian
statesmen, to the title.
THE OCEAN ISN'T AFIRE This remarkable photo shows destroyers of the British battle fleet in
the north Atlantic laying smoke screens. Seems as if the ocean were set aflre.
SLAIN WOMAN'S FAMILY ATTENDS COURT The children of Mrs. Clothilde Marchand, artist's
wife, arc photographed attending the trial in Buffalo, N. Y., of Lila Jimerson, Indian woman, charged
with causing Mrs. Marchand's death by having an old Indian woman murder her. Left to right: Mr. and
Mrs. George Marchand, Mr. and Mrs. Paul Marchand, Henrictte Marchand and Henry Marchand. At
right is Jim Crow, who alleges Lila tried to hire him to kill Mrs. Marchand,
WINNER OF LAGORCE TOUR
NEY A driving finish in the final
rounds gives Wild Bill Mclhorn
victory in the LaGorce open tour
ney at Miami Beach, Fla., nosing
outHorton Smith.
EASIER FOR COOLIES NOW ! Miss Betty Gordon, of Louisville, Ky., stopping off in Peipin, China,
on an around-the-world tour, brings about a revolution in Chinese street traffic. Feeling sorry for the
coolie pulling her rickshaw, she rented a bicycle for him. Now many of the rickshaw runners have
adopted the innovation. The photo shows Miss Gordon and her "invention"- in action.
rEEP, PEEP! A first glimpse of the outside world is obtained by this brood of chicks at Lawrence, Kas.

COOLIESIN JAMA ICA.
They Are Supplanting the Negroes
as Laborers.
WHY THE LITHE HANDSOME
BLACK PEOPLE FROM INDIA
CAME THERE.
How They Live Labor on the Great
Banana Plantations—How They Are
Paid and Oth6r Facts About Them.
The island of Jamaica is of special in
terest to Americans for two reasons. Its
unusual attractions as a winter resort
are becoming realized in our great north
ern cities, and its unusual advantages for
lucrative business in the cultivation
and export of tropical fruits are being
monopolized by American enterprise.
For nearly two centuries and a half,
ever since the days of Cromwell, Jamaica
has been an English provinca For near
ly two centuries, up to the freeing of
the slaves in 1840 and adoption of free
trade by England soon after, the island
was the home of rich and powerful plant
ers who did an enormous business in the
manufacture of sugar and rum. The time
of their greatest prosperity was at the be
ginning of this century. In 1805 was the
largest production of sugar, the estates
aggregating that year a total of 150,352
hogsheads. Rum, too, in 1806, reached
58,780 puncheons. The annual output
of sugar never fell below 100,000 hogs
heads until 1823 when the first anti-slav
ery agitation began. The slaves were
not finally freed till 1840. This loss of
their slave labor was a great blow to
the prosperity of the planters. But a se
verer blow occurred a few years later
when England adopted her free trade
COOLIE ivO.ltAN AND CHILD.
policy. In the slavery times the plant
ers were protected by the English gov
ernment, by a heavy differential duty.
But free trade reduced the price of sugar
one half to the English consumer, and
the Jamaica planters were unable to
withstand this second blow. With their
antiquated machinery they could not
compete with the planters of our South
ern States. Their estates became heavi
ly mortgaged anil many were abandoned.
In 1855 Jamaica’s production of sugar
was only 41,656 hogsheads, a striking
contrast to the 150,352 hogsheads pro
duced in 1805, half a century before.
The result has been that on the same
estates where formerly the Jamaica
planters raised their sugar cane and
reaped a golden harvest, by the aid of
slave labor and a protected market in
Europe, enterprising Americans are now
raising vast quantities of bananas and
coconuts and reaping a golden harvest in
masters. Therefore they were oppressed
by subjection to all possible taxes,
cheeks and disabilities until their resent
ment culminated in an ourbreak in 1865,
which resulted in an uprising in the
eastern part of the island and the murder
of all the officials in the little town of St.
Thomas in-the-East, and in horrible pil
lage, arson and bloodshed.
The uprising was, of course, immedi
ately put down, and with great severity,
by the English government, but it led
to reforms in the treatment of the negro
and was the beginning of Jamaica’s
present prosperity. The year of 1868 Was
SUNDAY DRESS AND WORKING DRESS.
notable one in Jamaican annals. It was
the dawning that came after the darkest
night; the year of the first surplus, of the
first shipment of fruit from Port An
tonio and the revival of coolie immigra
tion. The limits of this article do not
admit of any statement as to the rapid
development of the fruit trade by Ame
rican enterprise. Suffice it to say that
Port Antonio is a beautiful little town
on the northeastern end of the island aud
that it is the headquarters of the Boston
Fruit Company which controls 13 fruit
steamers which all the year round are
plying between our great northern cities
and Jamaica, and that each cargo is
worth from *10,000 to *20,000. At the
lowest calculation these steamers carry
away from Jamaica at least $00,000
worth of fruit every week and this is
only one of several groat American fruit
enterprises.
Arrived in Port Antonio the stranger
from the North who has never before
visited the Tropics will certainly be un
prepared, no matter how much ho has
road about it, to find so many black
people and so few white people. He had
understood that Jamaica was an Eng
lish province and had been for centuries,
but Engishmen or white men, of any
nationality, are as rare almost as negroes
in the arctics. At the first glimpse of
this little town, he realizes the real
meaning of the statement that Jamaica’s
population is 80 per cent, black, 17 per
cent, brown and three per cent, white.
Port Antonio’s streets are full of black
people. Negroes are doing everything
that is done in a small town. But the
stranger has been there but a little while
before ho discovers that there are other
black people in the town who are not ne
groes. He is very likely to meet a group
of these people coming into the town from
the country. The men are not so large as
the negroes, though some of them are as
talL They are slender, graceful and up
right in stature and as regular and hand
some in feature as the Anglo Saxon race
They wear only a loose white cloth, wrap
ped around the loins. Somo wear a sim
ple shirt and short trousers of the same
white cotton cloth. On their heads are
turbans. Several women aro nearly al
ways to be seen in these groups of hand
some black people. They aro always very
small and frequently very shapely in
form. They aro about five feet tall,
some shorter, few any taller. Many of
COOI.IIi HOMES ON BANANA PLANTATIONS.
the great markets of the United States.
Where once .tho lazy and ugly negro slave
toiled amidst the sugar cane with no
more rights than the beasts of burden,
and with even more cruel treatment, the
graceful and industrious coolie from
India now plants and harvests tho ba
nana crop, with compensation for his
labor and with legal redross from wrongs
and cruelty.
As in our own Southern states, so in
Jamaica,the freeing of the states resulted
in groat industrial diiturbance. The
negroes were, of courie, unfitted for use
fulness as free men. In that same year
of their freedom, 1810, 20,000 coolie la
borers from India were brought to Ja
maica. It whs expected that the coolie
would stand between tho planter and the
inconvenience and loss which he ex
perienced from'the intermittent industry
of the irresponsible freed negro.
But the ascendency of sugar and rum
was gone in Jamaica. The free negroes
wero a constant source of turmoil. They
were naturally hostile to the planters,
and since they outnumbered them 20 to
11. I’Vvo.v f©«n—i. |>v fnrnn- •.
Gliem are very handsome in feature, and
winning in expression of countenance,
indeed some ot them possess features as
lelicate in contour and noble in expres
sion as those of a beautiful American or
English girl.
Seldom does oue meet a group of these
people but that one or more of the
women is carrying a tiny naked baby,
which is always sitting astride of its
mother’s hip, and is thus held in place
by a gentle pressure of the mother’s arm
while its weight comes upon the hip.
Of course this method of carrying the
children induces an inclination of the
body to one sido, yet it seems never to in
luce a permanent deformity of the figure.
Elderly women who must have carried
many children in this way during their
lives are yet almost as erect, in figure as
bhe young girls.
These women are dressed in what is ap
parently one large piece of white cotton
doth. As shown in the illustration, it is
is if they had wrapped a sheet around
Ghem with such skill and held it there
with such an easy, nameless grasp and
.’race of bo:}” that, it “ “■**’ ^ oiimr of
itself, like the vine to the tree and is by
no means devoid of beauty and attractive
ness as a garment.
This group of people that we have met
coming into Port Antonio from the
country are the coolies from India, who
live and labor on the great banana plan
tations up in the valleys among the
mountains. It is worth while to visit
one of these inland valleys. From the
harbor of Port Antonio there is apparent
ly no level land anywhere, except the
narrow strip along the slioro, which is
occupied by the one main street of the
town and across which one could al
most throw a stone from tlio waters edge to
the stoop mountain side. But if you follow
the smooth, white limestone highway by
which the coolies entered the town, it will
lead you straight into the mountains and
their steep slopes, covered thick
ly with tropical verdure, rise up in fornt
and behind you, and now on this and
now on that are sloping downward or
upward, yet they always seem to recede
before that smooth white roadway that
winds and twists and advances and never
is too steep for a bicycle to climb it or
for horses to draw your carriage at an
easy trot. And almost before you know
it the mountains take flight from before
this fascinating victorious roadway, as if
they had abandoned their attempt to stop
its progress and were nut to utter rout,
and there spreads out before your de
lighted vision a glorious little tropical
valley with a brawling, rushing river
passing through its half dozen square
miles of level interval.
Well, is it named the (flnldpn "Vale
plantation, for from the river banks to
the mountain slopes on every side there
stretches a wonderful garden of what, to
our Northern eyes, appears to bo the
waving, rustling foliage of a curious
giant corn. But the white roadway soon
takes you down to the river and through
the plantation and you see that the
myriad banana palms are twice as high
and twice as big as tho biggest corn that
ever grow upon Iowa’s prairies. The
road leads to a little eminence on tho
other side of the valley and here are old
stono ruins covered with ivy, a modern
wooden dwelling and rows of white
washed sheds, with numbers on the doors
like so many bath houses. The ruins are
those of the old sugar mill and rum dis
tillery of tho days of long ago, when this
Golden Vale plantation was a prosperous
sugar estate. Tho modern dwelling is
tho home of the “busha” or mulatto
superintendent of the presont banana
plantation, and the rows of whitewashed
sheds are the homes of the coolie laborers.
As we sit down upon the piazza of the
“busha’s” house we learn that a com
pany of 20 or 30 coolies have just arrived
at the pantation from Iudia. Soon a
group of the graceful, handsome black
men comes up to the house. They stand
there smiling and chattering, while an
official, by the aid of an interpreter,
writes down their names in his book and
announces to them the work that they
must do and the penalties by way of fine
and imprisonment that will follow any
infringement of the rules.
These coolies are indentured by the na
tional government to labor in Jamaica
for the term of five years, under certain
conditions. During those five yoars they
are practically slaves to the provisions of
the contract, but they have their rights.
They come from India of their own free
will, and they are free to go home again
at the end of the five years. Also they
aro protected by regulation from cruel
treatment. The men receive one shilling
(or 25 cents) a day and tho women nine
ponce (or 18 cts.) Besides, each man re
ceives per day a pound and a half of rice,
a quarter of a pound of fish and a quarter
of a pound of peas, aud per week a pound
of salt and a pint of cocoanut oil. This
is of course much better than they could
possibly do in their own country.
As they stood thero, listening to the in
terpreter, their docile natures were ap
parent from their eager acquiescence with
the conditions that the official announced
to them. They are on this account and
becauso of their industry profeire l to the
negroes as regular employes upon the
plantations, though they are not so
strong physically as the negro, and can
not accomplish so much if the latter is
willing to exert himself.
Sometimes after the five years is over
the coolie has saved enough money to go
into business for himself in a small way
as a traveling peddler, sometimes ho re
turns home to India with his little sav
ings and lives and dies in his own
country a rich man, and sometimes he
contracts to labor for another five years.
The writer has seen several coolies who
woro aged men, as was evident from
tlieir wrinkles and long beards, streaked
with grey, though their figures were as
erect as those of their companions. Ho
also met and had some conversation with
one of those coolios who had served his
time as a laborer, who had married an
intelligent Creole woman. She kept a
small lodging house and supported her
handsome husband as a gentleman of
leisure, dressed in a salmon colored suit,
which apparently gave him so much de
light that ho was never weary of simply
wearing it and doing nothing else.
The coolio women, especially the older
ones, are noticeable especially because of
the burden of silver rings and coins and
ornaments that they carry about on
their wrists and ankles and necks. They
aro always of solid silver, aud sometimes
a woman will be the proud possessor of
30 or 40 of them, representing all the
wealth that she and her husband have
been able to gather together in a life
time. Less frequently a coolie woman
will bo seen wearing a gold button stuck
into or through one side of the nose.
This orna ment is often exquisitolv
carved. It is difficult to buy one of
these buttons, for they place a supersti
tious value upon them and, poor as they
aro, will not part with them except for a
very large sum.
The number of these coolies may be
grove of 63 acres. There an! thousands
and thousands of them in Jamaica, and
strange as it may seem, there is no con
flict between them and the negroes. The
latter are so lazy and arc so amply pro
vided for by nature for their simple
necessities of life that they regard with
no jealousy or alarm the encroachments
of their coolies upon the Held of labor.
They simply look down upon them with
lofty contempt as little better than slaves.
Of course some negroes work upon the
plantations, and nearly all the labor in
the towns is done by negroes, and many
raise bananas and sell them independent
ly, hut they cannot bo depended upon
for the performance of the arduous and
steady labor necessary on the fruit plan
tations.
The coolies work among the banana
palms entirely naked, except for the
loose cloth around the loins, as shown in
the illustration. Though so exposed to
the dangerous bite of the tarantula that
is so numerous among the 1 bananas, yet
it is seldom that they are ever bitten.
Some of the more enterprising of the
coolie laborers learn to adopt the English
style of dress, and on special occasions
are proud wearers of a well fitting suit of
clothes. E. H. E.
MR. BEECHER’S WIDOW.
At S3 She is Getting Heady fora Trip to
Puget Sound.
Mrs. Henry Ward Beacher will be 8A
years old on August 26, but before that sh
will make a journey of 6.000 milos across
the continent and back. Instead of look
ing upon the trip of seven days each
way with dread, she regards it rather as
gv/XJJg DU j_ ugUl
Sound to visit her son Herbert Beecher,
who is a pilot for the revenue cutters in
the service there. “Herbert is my baby,’
she said yesterday; “my forty-one-year
old baby,” weigliing'220 pounds. Pretty
good for Ja baby isn’t it? I haven't beer
out there in four years, and there is a
member of the family I haven’t seen and
1 want to see hor.
“It’s a little lonesome here, too, when
most of my friends and neighbors are
out of town. I leave here on the 20ti
with my grandson. Samuel Scoville,
who has just completed his law course in
Now York. The journey takes seven days
but they let me have a stateroom, and I
just go on as usual, reading or sewing or
passing the time as I have a mind to,
and not bothering about what they haye
for dinnor until thoy ctill me. I have no
fears of the journey, and I expect to en
joy it and be back here in a few days
more than a month. ’ ’
Much has been written regarding Mrs.
Beecher’s opposiion to the substitution
of a new hymnal for the one used in Ply
mouth church in her /husband’s days.
She said yesterday;
2 “All there was of it was that I told Dr
Abbott I wished he would let the
hymnal alone while I lived. Ho said that
300 hymns in it wero never sung, and lie
wanted to get rid of them. I asked him
if all the hymns in the other hymn books
used all around the city, wero sung, and
when he said ‘No,’ I said I thought it
was not then of such importance that
ours be amended immediately and that I
thought it might be allowed to stand as
it was during my life. That was all
tliero was of it, except that I told him
Mr. Beecher had been engaged upon re
vising the hymnal, omitting some of the
hymns, and had just completed the work
wheil he died. Dr. Abbott asked if
I could get those papers, and I gave them
to him, but he did nor use them. His
son is a very fine musician, and the new
book contains some hymn music written
by him, and it is very nice; only I felt
that I should like the book to remain un
changed while I lived. N. Y. gun.

escape from death
to-day, my dear. I
ate one of those lawn
mower signs and in
process of digestion
it cut me several

you always were
careless and impru
dent in eating. What

"And do you sot find life in so small

Allce's-titay- In Boston passed rapidly

"Ob, plesae do not ask that." replied

That Is nothing," answered Alice,

"t will not promise. I will see how

When, thnt evening camo Blanch

nj? and the reserve had worn away,

Come, now, dear, just one little song;
only one to plenso me." At first Alice
thought to refuse, but her pride came to
the rescue, and the feeling that she
would show hr friend Hint slu wmh

moment's hesitation she seated herself
and sang "The Last Uose of Summer."
She hud sung It many, many times be
fore, and every trill and exquisite quiv
er of its pathos was as familiar to her
as the music of the brook where she
had played in childhood. She sang as
she never hnd before, nnd to an au
dience that listened entranced. When
the Inst sweet note bad passed her red
lips, she arose quickly and returned to

Mrs. Nason's eyes, to bo quickly brush
ed away with a priceless bit of lnce.
Sweet Alice, the motherless little coun
try girl, had from that moment enter
ed the henrt of Mrs. Nuson. When the
nppluuse bad subsided, it was Frank
that next pleaded.
"Won't you sing one for me now,

the music rnck "Ben Bolt!"
"But I ouly consented to sing once
for Blanch," Alice replied, "and there
are others here who I am sure can do
much better."
'Come, please," he said conxlngly,
"just this one for me." And once more
Alice touched the keys.
Back to a simply furnished parlor
In Sandgate, with its lamp on the
piano and open Are burning brightly
