that company In thought, and maybe
others there, whose youth had been
among country scenes, were carried
back to them by the singer's voice and
saw a bywny schoolhouse "nnd a shad
ed nook by a running brook" In fancy,
or perhaps' a little white stone in some
grass grown corner, where, "obscure
and alone," lay a boyhood's sweet
heart! All the pathos of our lost youth
trilled In the voice of Alice Page as she
song that old, old song. Not one in
that little audience but was enthralled
by the winsome witchery of her voice
and for the moment was young again
In thought and feeling. When the
guests had departed Mrs. Nason turned
to Alice and, tuking her face in her
bands, exclaimed, "I want to kiss the
Hps that have brought tears to my
eyes tonight"
The last evening of her visit Blie de-
elded to spend with her brother, and
when she came to bid adieu to her
hostess that much dreaded haughty
mother had resolved herself Into a
charming lady.
"It is odd, Bertie," she said to her
brother that evening when they were
alone together, "how different people
seem when one comes to know them.
From one or two things which you have
said and an admission that Frank
made a year ago I felt I should be sure
to hate his mother, and now I think
she is perfectly lovely."
"So she Is to those she likes," an
swered Albert. "You carried her heart
by storm last evening as well as the
rest of the company. I never beard
you sing so well."
"I am glad I didn't breok down, any
wuy," she replied, "for when I touched
the piano my heart seemed In my
mouth."
For an hour they discussed the Na
sons, while Albert noticed his sister
avoided any mention of Frank, and
then he said: "Well, sis, which of the
places we have looked at do you think
I best engage, nnd when will you be
ready to move?"
Alice pursed her lips and looked at
the shipwreck scene near her as If It
contained a revelation.
"I am not so sure," she answered
finally, "that we should make the
change at present. It I were certain
your beautiful waif of the sea -would

Alice's blue eyes assumed an ex

face the placidity of a mill pond as
she answered, "I had quite forgotten
his existence!"

"She has a beautiful figure and eyes

It is recognized that emigration.
can seldom act as a complete remedy
for the excessive growth of popula
tion. Its effects in this direction
vary greatly from country to county
according to its proportion to the
total population, but it never equals,
and rarely approaches, the percent
age of the excess of the birth-rate
over the death-rate. In India its
effect is infinitesimal ou the vast
population which finds itself in a sort
of cul de sac whence there is no going
backwards towards the north or
vest, aurt the only escape is by sea
There is still room for relief of con'
gested districts by Internal migration
though, so far, the pressure of
short food supply has not yet reach
ed a severity sufficient to overcome
the innate inclination of almost all
Indians to ftay at home. Religious
objections to movement beyond sea
may have operated to some extent
but the real cause checking emigra
tion is the conservative nature of
the people end their love of the
locality in which ?they and their au
cestors have lived and ploughed, o
worked, for generations past.
It is true that Indian merchants

traha, but their numbers are com

belong to th more enterprising class

of Bombay. Emigration to Ceylcn
or the Straits Settlements can hardly
be counted true iroigration, for these
countries are almost part of Indi
itself, and those who go to them
generally only do so temporarily

In his power to restrict the emigra

The general position which has been

THE WEST INDIAN IN LONDON.
Dr. Griffin’s lecture before the Society of
Arts may be highly useful as an elaborate ad
vertisement of a particular sagar refivery, and
it certainly contained some interesting informa
tion. The makers of good raw sugars will
hardly thank him, however, for his studied
depreciation of their produce. Itis something
quite new to learn that sugar, which in regard
ot look, and taste, and apparcot purity, is quite
fit to g 0 immediately into consumption without
the intervention of the refiner in this country,
is yet most objectionable” Tt contains a lot of
deleterious substances, according to the lectur
er. Bat when he desceribed the monster that
lurked in the innscent-looking sugir, and show
ed the photograph of a thing ¢ with eight
jointed and spiny legs armed with formidable
hooks,” he fairly scut a shudder through his
audience. And when he said that in the sugar
supplied to a Dablin workhouse there were
100,000 of these monsters to a pound of sugar,
the miond of his audience, already sufliciently
confused between the dry scientific details and
the pretty pictures of the magic lantern, was
completely prostrated. A pauper cawv, no
doubt, stand a good deal, bat to have to swal
low in every pound of sugar he eats 800,000
jointed and spiny legs (to say nothing of
bodies) armed with formidable hooks, must
iovest poverty with additional terrors. The
faot is that good raw sugar that comes from
some of the West India colonics, and is import
ed in a condition ostensibly fit for direct con
samption, is quite as good and pure, aund cer
tainly more sweet, and therefore, more econo
mical to use than a great deal of white sugar
that is turned out by the refineries in this coun
try. Os course, the lecturer’s depreciation of
raw sugar, was appropriately followed by a
pretty illumioated picture of a refinery, where
all the evil qualities wero extracted from this
kind of produce, and all the nasty things it
contained, according to the lecturer, were
killed. The audience would have been stlil
more delighted if they had been allowed to
witness a pictorial representation of a terrific
combat betwecn the pauper and the acarus
sacchari, the latter duly magnified on the
sereen, in proportion to the auimal’s rhetorical
sige’ in thelecturer’s address. It may bo as
sumed. that mobody will use a pound of good
raw sugar the less on account of the seientifie
sensationalism which. marred an otherwise ad
mirable lecture.
. That delightful piece of diplomacy called the
Sugar Duties and Drawbacks Convention has
still another year to run. The French, who

which they have never carricd out, they are
prevented from adopting that very reform
which will most effectually accomplish the
ohject of the Treaty itself. The Assembly
has passed the law for establishing refining in

in Bond is a good thing, asisadmitted by the
Freneh Government and Assembly, why not
establish it at once? The English Govern

i diately adopt a system by which the Krench
l Treasury sha'l save itself from an cnormous
and growing loss. There 13 no reason to sup
l pose that the other contracting prwers would
1 refuse their consent. The esisting Convantion,
| therefore, is no obstacle at all to France doing
| at once an act of great advantage to herself and
’ of simple justice to this county.

The annual report on the [mmigration De
partment in Jamaica for the year 1872-3 came
home ULy the last mail. During the season
named there were four ships, eirrying 1,062
Indian immigrants. The mortality on board
was 2.81. Thne coolics are all fine healthy
immiarants, especially those by the Stock
hrideoe, who are considered the finest body of
Indians yet introduced into the colony. The
coolios located in Clarendon soon became able
to take care of themselves, and engage actively
in estates’ work. All were in a coudition to be
taken off rations at the expiration of the first
three months required by law, and to be allow
ed to rececive the whole of their earnings in
money. The coolies who arrived in 1863 had,
in the year under report, completed their ten
years’ industrial residence, and it isa cheering
and noteworthy fact that they all gzave up their
back passage, and clected to remain as perma
nent colonists. The general conduect of the
immigrant population has been good, the con
vietions for criminal offences have bcen few.
In point of health the year 1872-3 was rather
sickly, and the mortality was a little higher,
being 3.06 against 2.45 in 1871-2. The pro
portion of births among the indentured immi
grants shows a steady increase, being, in
1872-3, 2.27 agaiust 1.78 in the previous year.
The following are the closing sentences of Mcr.
Alexander’s valuable and interesting report :
“The time-expired immigrants in Jamaica
may be described as a most thriving class; and I
miy mention, as an instance of the saccess to
which an industrious coolic can attain in this
country, the case of *Secepersunsing,” who ar
rived here in the ship Themis in 1860. This
man recently became the proprietor of ¢ Brien’s
Pen,” in the parish of Hanover, for which he
—piid 600 Z, and sabseqaently has purchased
another run of land for 2002, This is not a
solitary case, for everywhere throughout the
country the time-expired coolie may be found
in comparatively affluent circumstances. It
may be interesting to state that, in the district
of Westmoreland alone, during the past year,
the amount of 2407. has been paid in taxes by
time-expired immigrants. In conclusion, I am
glad to say that the general treatment of the
immigrants continues to be most satisfactory ;
care has been taken in providing them with
good houses, and their earnings have been re
galarly paid. Many of the indentured coolies
are now depositors in the Government savings
banks throughout the country. It is with
pleasure that I am able to state, that in no case
matters requiring adjestment have ever remain
ed unattended to when once brought to the
notice of the employer by the officers of the
department; and I may add, that in every
instance where the treatment of coclies is good,
and the employer understands how to manage
them, they have given satisfaction.”
The month of May is always the time for a
tremendous display of conseious virtue in London.
There are two ways in which people can show
their superior excellence —first, by openly pre
claiming the fact themselves, and sccondly by
attacking their neighbours, and the consequence
| is that more vanity and ill-nature find expression
during the merry mounth than at any other period
of the year. Philanthropic members of Parlia
l ment lose their voice, and expend the remains of
their intelligence in the glorious and exciting
l work. One nced not look for argument or for a
| calim statement of facts on such occasions. A
| broad representation is all that is necessary.
{ Take, for instance, the statement made (accord
ine to the Times) at the meeting of a body of
peopla who call themselves the Kborigines‘ Pro
| teetion Society, that ¢ coolie emigration to the
i Mauritins and other places was a modified form
| of Slavery.” A pretty sentence wherewith to
tickle the cars of a May Mecting. and rouse its
! delicate sensibilities. COf course, any argument
or any recapitulation of facts would bo wasted
] upon a society which continues to indulge in
| talk.of this kind, and the choice sentenco quoted
| above will amuse some people in the tropies who
| may happen to know a little about coolie emigra
( tion. Whatoever may be the condition of the
| Chinese in Cuba, there is no excuse whatover for
| applying a prejudicial epithet to Indian emigra
| tion to British colonies. This system of emigra
| tion is good for all concerned, for the coolie him
| self most of all. Why, the Indian immigrants on
| some estates in Demerara are actually subserib
| ing out of their carnings towards the fund for
| relieving their starving countrymen in Bengal.
| They know that they would have been starving
| themsclves if they had never omigruted to a place
| where they could find plenty o food and ample
| wages, and in trying to help their suffering
brethren in their own country they are solting an
example of charity and good feeling that might
well bo followed, in some important respects, by
| people who are in the habit of claiming, whilo at
| the same time they abusé, the graceful title of
philanthropists.
; The further papors relative to guano deposits
| in Peru open with a despatch from Admiral
| Cochrane repeating his suggestion to the Lords of
| the Admiralty that a party of civil engineers
| should be sent out from England to make a tho
' rough scientific survey of the deposists in Peru.
| Commander Cookson estimates the guano in the
I deposits at Tarapaca at 7,400,000 tons. The
| commander, in his report, gives somne interesting
| information as to his mode of surveying and
| bases of caleulation, and a plan is attached which
| conveys an idea of the manner in which the
’ guano lies. Where does all this mass of guano
| come from, and what is the length of time re
' quired to account for its existence, are questions
| which have not yet been determined. Sea-lions
] and seals have contributed largely to many of
the deposits, as, in working the guano, a great
quantity of bones of seals are found; but tho
deposits of * white guano™ are formed solely by
birds. A native who had been long resident at
| Pabellon de Pica informed Commander Cookson
| that forty years ago the whole promontory was
| covered with birds (pelican, gannet, &e.) in
| countless numbers. A plague subsequently visit
| ed them, and they died literally by millions, the
| whole shore for miles being strewed with their
bodics, and now scarcely a bird is to be seen.
That a better market should result from the
abolition of the sugar duties was naturally to be
expected. And there secems no reason why the
slight improvement at present manifest should not
be still further developed. It is most essential
that accurate statistics should be obtained of
imports and stocks, and now that the Govern
ment have nothing to do with sugar as an article
of revenue, the difficulty in the way of obtaining
trustworthy figures is increased to some extont.
The subject is worthy the attention of importers,
as well as of that department of the Government
which compiles and issues the monthly returns.
A production slightly less and a probably increas
ed consumption open a prospect of a steady
market for some time. The latest advices from
| Havana pointto a deficiency, owing to the pro
tracted drought, of 20 to 25 per cent in the yield.
The same may be said of Mauritius as a conse
quence of the hurricane. The Porto Rico crop,
now drawing to a close, is expected to be a third
short of last year’s production, and planters are
said to be anxious as to the succeeding'cmf.
which is endangored by the dry weather. In
putting forward any anticipations of a better
market, it must always be remembered that. they
can hardly be fully realised until. stocks in the
United Kingdom, which are still very heavy in
comparison with this time last year, show &
sensible decline.

Farming in America,
(Practical Farmer.)
A popular topic at the present day is
agricaltural depression. Many farmers are
discouraged, prices are low, and competition
ig sharp. The style of living adopted by
farmers, and which seems to them indis
pensable, is far moce expensive than that
of ou. fathers, and the machinery required
tocarry on successfully a farm of a hun.
dred acres, now costs so much as to be a
heavy tax on the farmer. G anted, that few
farmers are getting rich, they may still be
doing better and have their funds more
sately invested than they would be likely
to do in any other business. There is to
my mind a bright side to farm life, and 1
would muaeh rather write about it than the
gloomy side, and I will devote this paper
to pointing out some of the unacknowiedg
ed profits and compensations of farm life.
Does not the farmer make a mistake when
he fails to count as a legitimate part of the
profit of the farm that which it-contributes
to the support and comfort of his family ?
1t seems to me that there is no other way
in which an investment of four or five thou
sand dellars can be made to combine with
perfect safety so large a return of eom
fort, and so certain sapport ‘for the fam
-Ily as by putting it into a farm. Os course
there cau 92 mismanagement on the farm
as well ag any where else, but with intelli<
gent management and industry, the farmer
can manage to make a good livilg and to
@o it without that constant stiuggle and
anxziety which most men of small capital ex
perience in the making of a hiving.
Not only is the farmer’s living .sure, but
he can always have the best the land fur.
nishes if be chooses, and if h+ does not de
this it is usnally because he doss not live
up to bhis privileges. Iliveon a farm of
ninety acres, sixty of which is good plow
Jand and the remainder permanent -blne
grass pasture, supplied with pure, spring
water. Lbave a good.eottage with eleven
o Vg aml D R e aed e
roons, not counting halls or Summaoe
Licehen ; and to rent as goml a oree withy
as pleasant surroundings in a town of Iwy
or three thonsamd irnabitants, would cost
from fifteen to twenty dollars a montr. A
excel'ent tree taropike passes my door and
I can drive to eliareh ov post office in twen
tv minutes. This farm with its improve
ments, has for the last ten yoars, been
valued on the tax duplicate at a little less
than four thousand dolars, and this has
been reduced considerably by the decennial
appraisement: Our rate qf taxation in the
country is always much less than in towu,
and is rarely muctr above 1 per cent., 80
that my taxes on the farmy and a thousand

never exceeded S7O a year, aud are usually
less. Now let us see what this farm furn
ishes my family in addition to a home.
Firsty ovr breaustuft, my family averages
eight persons, and our flour, corn meal and.
buckwhea. flour all come from the farm,
and would eost if purchused an average of
about $35 a year. Our fruit, which the farn

year, as we have a constant aml liberal
sapply of small fruits,beginninzwith straw
berries, followed by raspberries, biack
berries and grapes, and ovchard fiui s in
abundance. We do not make a quact
or two of berries last a day, but dur
ing the strawberry season now just clos
ed, we ate a peck of strawberries a
day most of the time, aud they were
fresh from the vines aud better than
we could have Dbought in the market.
We keep three or four Jersey cows, priuei
pally for the family, although we sell soing
buftter, but not until the family has had all
the butter, eream jand milk they can use,
and in addition my wife makes a supply of
cheese during the hot weather when butter
is plenty and cheap. You may put down
a credit to the farm of §6O for dairy pro
ducts; but that is far less than the saws
quality and amvount would cost if'we should”
bay it, for my family uses about oie pounil
of butter a day, and this at 20 cents a

Are fresh laid eggs and Spring chickens &
desirable addition to the Lill of fare? Well,
we have a eonstant supply of these the year

I think we will gredit our poultry yard

Buat lam not done yet, for do we not

Winter? There is not a week in the en

nish us good meats, and we will give it =

My investment is safe. No bank cashier

and lam gradaally improving wmy farm.
Now if [ should grow discontented and sell
out I could not get the true value of tho
property in these times of discoursgement,
biit it I shonld find a customer who would
give me $5,000 ¢'sh for my farm, teams
and tools, how eould I iuvest the money so
that it wouald be sale, and yield the com
fort to my family it does now? There are
millions of capital seeking inves:ment, the
owners of which would be g'ad to loan it
on good security ‘at six per cent. We will
sappose that I could do this and then my
income wouald be 8300 a year. It would

the capital to buy it. Isl start in sowme
new business I put myselt in cowpetition
with men of training aid experience, and’' I
lose much of the experience I have gained.
Whercfore I conclude that the words of
Shakespeare apply to my casa when Ny
said, “Tis better ro endurs the ills we liave
than fiy to others that we know not of%

(Prepared cj the National Geographic So
ciety, Washington, D. C.)
Sumatra, fourth largest island in
the world outside the frozen polar
regions, although extremely fertile
and rich in resources, is not nearly so
well developed as Java, its smaller
neighbor among Holland’s huge east
ern colonies. These islands lie in
the heart of the tropics where weather
and vegetation conspire under or
dinary circumstances to make man a
lazy, happy-go-lucky creature, willing
to take things as they come and like
ly to look upon labor as a quite un
necessary pastime. In Java, however,
so great has become the pressure of
population, the people must work like
beavers, and they have made their
Island a garden spot. Sumatra has
only about 3,000,000 Inhabitants in its
162,000 square miles and there the
sluggishness toward development that
the tropics tends to breed is evident.
The fact, too, that Sumatra’s natives
are nearer savagery and th&t they
have resented the Intrusion of their
Dutch masters has held the island
back.
But as Java’s population becomes
more pressing, Sumatra looms up near
by as a land of greater and greater
promise, and despite savage tenden
cies, Dutch control and Dutch capital
are becoming more firmly settled in
the Island. The development is es
pecially taking the form of establish
ment of great rubber and tobacco plan
tations. The warlike Sumatrans are
not available for labor, and in the
development of the country practically
all the labor has to be Imported on
short-term contracts. Chiefly it is
Chinese, which Is expensive; Kling,
which is viewed with disfavor by the
British Indian government, or Java
nese, which is unwilling to come and
does not thrive In the climate.
The tribulations of a labor contrac
tor from the time of collecting his
gang to their final delivery in Suma
tra are legion and, to one disinterest
ed, very amusing.
The Javanese is tractable and physi
cally a fair laborer, but neither very
ambitious nor reliable. He likes his
feast days, his rice harvesting, his lit
tle comforts and luxuries, and is not
eager to forego them for the uncertain
inducements of foreign lands. But his
mind is receptive and the clever con
tractor, fortifying It with well-chosen
stories of fortunes easily made, belit
tling the coolie’s fears and objections,
is often able to secure his contract by
the timely offer of a new sarong (the
chief article of dress worn in the Ma
lay archipelago) and perhaps a
month’s wages in advance.
Woes of the Labor Contractor.
But here the contractor’s troubles
begin. Unless carefully guarded, the
coolie’s enthusiasm Is very apt to
wane, and the moment for departure
arrives with coolie, new sarong and
month’s wages unaccounted for.
Even when safely gathered on board
ship and the coast of Java has been
sunk, there remains still to be cleared
the intervening port of Singapore.
Thera, In disguise, wily touts for the
Malayan coolie brokers smuggle them
selves aboard, Bo matter how vigilant
the ship’s officers may fee, for labor is
everywhere in demand. With much
astuteness they proceed to poison the
minds of the already frightened Suma
tra-bound Javanese. •
“Sumatra? A country of tigers and
ferocious savages who eat nothing but
coolies; a cold land, where there is
no sun, no rice; where laborers are un
paid, cruelly treated, and whence they
rarely return 1”
So the tout whispers on, adding ter
ror to their premonitions, refuting all
that the contractor had said, and in
the end offering to aid in their imme
diate escape from the horrible fate irf
store to the tempting security of for
tune and happiness in the Malay
states.
Strict watch is kept over the ship
while In Singapore, but scarcely a trip
'.s taken that a few of those under con-
Live Stock Dealer of Sumatra.
tract are not among the missing when
the final count is made. For every
one lost the first mate Is personally
fined, about fifty gulden; but if he
brings a certain percentage safely to
their destination he receives a liberal
bonus.
Arrived at a Sumatran port, the
coolies pass single file down the gang
way between two officers and a con
tractor’s agent, who check them as
they go—men, women, boys and girls,
with folded mats under their arms and
their possessions tied up in long cloths
slung around their necks and resting
on their hips. Only those with babies
are kept apart and counted last, lest
one tiny head should be overlooked.
They are a picturesque lot in their
gay-colored clothes. Most of the wom
en go bareheaded, their black hair
brushed back and knotted behind, with
strings of coral beads hanging around
their necks and big buttons of gold
and silver, jade, amber or ebony ex
tending from their pierced ear-lobes.
Brilliant scarves half conceal their
fresh white corsages, and leather belts
wltn massive silver buckles encircle
sarongs of many hues.
Around the heads of nearly all the
men one finds twisted the universal
brown kerchiefs of Java, flaunting
starched corners; and, in addition to
their sarongs and a few short coats
and pajama tops, there is a noticeable
partiality for white undershirts and
long pink drawers.
Fine Rubber and Tobacco Land.
The entire east coast of Sumatra is
low and flat, an alluvial plain formed
by silt washed down from the moun
tains by an extremely heavy rainfall.
In this way the whole of the broad
plain between mountains and sea,
which, behind its mangrove fringe,
forms the splendidly rich lands of rub
ber and tobacco estates, has gradually
been built up and is steadily being ex
tended.
The mangrove plays a considerable
part in this extension because of its
remarkable powers of reproduction.
Growing partly in the shallow water
of the littoral, these trees spread out
a labyrinth of surface roots that aV!t
as a framework for the accumulating
mud, which in the course of time rises
above the surface and forms land.
The shallow water and shifting mud
banks of the coast make the location
of ports unreliable and frequently ne
cessitate their removal or abandon
ment after they have once been estab
lished.
Although large steamers now dock
in the port of Dell, like most other Su
matran ports it is but a broad, mud
colored stream winding sluggishly
through dense equatorial swamps. A
railroad enters this port, largely on
stilts, and carries one some miles be
fore he is really ©n solid ground.
Medan, capital of the government of
the east coast of Sumatra, is 14 miles
inland from Dell. It is a modern
town, created by the Dutch and laid
out in an attractive manner.
There is an airy appearance and a
cheerful “white man’s” atmosphere
about the official buildings around Its
spacious square and the cool, shaded
streets of its European quarter.
The white bungalows are extremely
attractive in their green and well-kept
grounds, shaded by tall royal palms,
rubber trees, bamboo, banyans,
“flames of the forest,” travelers’ trees
and other tropical growth.
The huge buildings of the Dell com
pany, with * European hospital and a
well-appointed asylum for native im
migrants, are almost hidden in the
dense verdure of a park filled with
beautiful shade trees. Farther out
are the native compounds and various
Asiatic quarters.
Medan’s prosperity and importance
are due to its location in the center of
the rich tdbacco lands; and, owing to
this, with the consequent demand for
labor and to the scarcity of native Su
matrese, its population of about 14,000
is a ver}' mixed one.
A Goocr Listener
“Does this parrot talk?”
“No —but he is really a very fine lis
tener.”
You never hear a man boast that his
wife shines his shoes.

Published Daily except Sunday

Nome. Alaska, July 1-', 1010.
ANTIMONY.
A miner in Nome is in receipt oi
a communication irom a smelter
company in San Francisco wnereiu
it is stated that the market for an
timony ore lias dropped so badiy
cnly $15 or $20 can be offered for
the highest grades of the ore.
•rne firm states that it is itn
possible to compete with China
■which is now supplying the market
■with enormous quantities of the
ore and unless the Alaskan ore can
tie got out at the above price they
cannot handle it.
Naturally under existing condi
tions there will be no attempt made
to handle antimony ore in this sec
tion of the country.
This matter is worth more than
temporary consideration.
In the first place it reveals the
fact that industry is assuming a
more and more international nature.
The good republican protectionist
will right away declare that the only
remedy for fthte state of things is
to put on a duty on this ore. They
will blame democratic free trade for
the Chinese competition.
But suppose they did place a
heavy ad valorem duty upon this
antimony, it is evident that the ma
nufacturers using the ore would
then have to pay more for their
antimony, and the product of their
plants would, with the increase in
raw material, be placed at a disad
vantage with the plants which in
other countries received the anti
mony, and the product of their
plants would, with the increase in
raw material, be placed at a disad
vantage with the plants which in
other countries received the anti
mony free of duty.
Protection as a policy would be
effective from an employer's point
of view were American trade con
fined solely within domestic limits.
Then all would benefit to the extent
that the antimony mine owner
would benefit, because the Amer
ean manufacturer would be protect
ed in the American market against
j<<>ods made from cheaper antimony
and the like.
But these are days of world trade.
The raw material such as antimony
ore and the like must be had as
cheaply as possible so that Amer
ican industries utilizing such will
not be at a disadvantage compared
with those using the cheaper raw
material. The manufactured goods
of one and the other will meet in
the same plane on the foreign mar
kets in this event and not other
wise. It is then that other indus
trial advisers suggest breaking down
the American standard of wages
to that of other countries, so that
the American manufacturer will be
at no like disadvantage in this re
gard.
These are the great industrial
questions which the system is de
veloping within the womb of the
present, for profit making of the
future.
It was all very well just as long
as the Americans could by improved
machinery and by speeding up and
efficiency counterbalance in produc
tiveness of the few the cheap labor
of the many coolies in oth r coun
tries. But those days are gone by.
The modern Chinaman uses machin
ery and modern tools. He is build
ing hydro-electric plants, machine
drills, steam, compressed air, elec
tric cranes and all the wonderful
improvements in technology, many
of which Americans dont know a
thing about.
For the past ten years technical
advance has been more marked In
Europe than America. Only Amer
icans living in the shadows of the
setting past think otherwise. The
American mechanical engineer
knows better, and he is struck with
the marvels of the later internal
combustion engines, the chemical
developments and the applications
of the ast thing in science to in
dustry. Most of these arc being
adapted in Japan, in China and in
India. So that in addition to the
cheapest labor In the world and the
most simple standard of living, the
orientals thanks to Caucasian in
struction, are now utilizing the lat
est technical improvements in the
industries.
Take this antimony ore mining
When the Chinese toos. over control
and operation of the Kuangski pro
\ Vince mines, they first of all intro
duced over a quarter million dollars
\ "worth of the very latest machinery.
Nothing in Butte or the Rand or in
^Colorado is more modern or more
such thing as competing with China
n the antimony market.
As anyone can see at a glance,
this condition is extending to almost
avery line. It is ceveioping in
Japan in the iron and steel trade
It is evolving in China in the cement
textile and other industries.
China is dumping pig iron on the
I acific Coast. With a score of high
ly capitalized cement plants along
the coast of Washington and Cali
fornia, Hongkong is dumping a
better grade of cheaper cement
along the Pacific Coast which is the
despair of competitors.
It is unnecessary to add details.
Anyone that reads with any atten
tion trade journals or the reports as
to Oriental imports can fill in facts
by the score.
What then? Chinese exclusion
or other anti-immigration laws are
no use to prevent American labor
being placed in direct com
petition with the labor
of the Orient. If the manufactured
goods of Pittsburg and Canton meet
in the market of South Amevica,
Africa or Australia, It means that
American labor wntch produced
those goods meets in competition
therein also.
There is no possibility of getting
over this. It is a fact. And the at
tempt of trade unionists to keep out
members of oriental races from
their organization is only an amus
ing attempt to prevent a competi
tion that exists and will exist none
the less.
Furthermore just so surely as a
coolie standard of labor exists In
any part of the world, in these days
of world trade and world markets,
just so surely will the tendency be
to iower the American standard *o
that of the coolie. We see that
actually happen where America
comes in direct competition with
lower wage scales, and it is witness
able in the instinctive demands of
industrial chiefs, educators, econom
ists when they urge the American
worker to a cheaper mode of living,
a thriftier habit of life and a greater
contentment with frugal fare. Dieti
cians are urging that vegetable and
cereal fare tie substituted for meat
and costlier foods, so that in turn
the subsistence standard being
lowered, the wages which as a gen
eral rule hover around the subsist
ence standard, may lower also.
Very obviously such a condition
of things will not be accomplished
without a struggle. The workers
in America, slow to move and leth
argic as can be well Imagined, will
resent the efforts to reduce them
entirely to the coolie standard. To.
day their instinct repels the notion,
but their lack of understanding due
to the misrepresentation by so-called
labor leaders, leads them to support
reforms in the way of exclusion and
restriction of immigration, to dally
with high toriffs add the like as it
their future salvation rested in any
of these things.
But clearly also, the workers will
be subjected to such economic for
ces when they know they are the
producers for a world market that
they willl demand better conditions
than the exigencies of the foreign
market will permit being given
them
The American manufacturer Is
placed today in a peculiar position.
His domestic market while the best
he has got is wholly unequal to the
demands of the industries.
Foreign markets are necessary to
his population. He cannot thrive
Into the millloned class unless ho
finds an outlet for his steel, his
lumber, his machinery, his tools, his
structural Iron, his notions of all
kinds. Yet on those foreign mark
ets he has to compete with nations
just as ingenious and just as able as
he. And they have their coolie la
bor, and it is up to him to make
coolies of his laboring classes if he
would succeed in meeting his for
eign rival on an equal footing.
He probably will appeal to the
workers in a friendly mood to bear
with reduced wages from a patri
otic sense, just as the railroad
building commission in Alaska
sought Alaskans patriotically to ac
cept low wages becauso the railpoad
was a patriotic work. He will prob
ably employ his proachers to incul
cate visions of apother world upon
the minds of the tollers, so that
they will be satisfied to burn stink
sticks to the Josses instead of ask
ing for more pay; he will utilize
the movies and the schools and the
entire educational system to teach
that the workers should be satisfied
with very little so that America
ghould lead first in industry
throughout the world.
These tactics are likely to fail,
but against open resistance by that
time the preparedness program will
have been fully carried out. Com
pulsory militarism will be an ac
complished fact and any attempt at
resistance will be suppressed with
vigor and with rigor. That is the
real purpose of preparedness.
All of this theory of living, it
may be observed, is built upon the
foundation that the manufacturing
interests are the only interests
worth considering. That is, toe, the
prevalent idea. It is conceded by
all employers that the employing
class represents the nation. it is
for them, and General Wood told
them so plainly, that armies are
created r id that wars are fought,
it certainly is around the enter
prise of commerce, the activities of
industry, the manipulations of
finance that government activities
develop. The tariff makes and un
makes administrations; currency
bills elect and defeat politicians;
consular staffs are maintained for
business expansion and the highest
fun'-tion of the navy is to protect
the flag of trade. These facts are
so obvious that they scarcely need
emphasis. The better class of citi
sen is the richer class and the poor
er are certainly the less responsible,
l’he court listens to a Rockefeller
with deference; a Morse is pardoned
because his health is indifferent;
vices still loom large through tat
ters, and “robes and furred gowns
hide all”. The leading society man
is also the man with large bank
account; the prominent church
member is seldom the laborer. The
few who are well fixed are those
for whom legal, social, political con
ditions are adjusted.
Likely enough then society will
accept as a condition the facts that
social conditions today emphasize;
that the rest of its members exist
for the benefit of the few who own.
Possibly the idea that the great
est good of the greatest number
should be the worthy ideal of so
ciety's activities may In time pene
trate. But not until the world of
profit- making has been subjected to
considerable disturbance.

NEW ADVERTISEMENT.

mas—Mr. Robert Long, Rebecca Joseph
& child.
FREDERIKSTED.

Thomas—Mr. George Flynn & sister Miss
Alice Flyun, Adella Hatchett, Sarah
Ventor, Jane Petersen, Irnestine James,
Jos. Nathawiel.
Passengers Left,
CHRISTIANSTED.
July 24th Per schr «Vigilant” for St.
Thomas—Dr. C, E. Taylor, Mavia Patrick
& 2 children.
. FREDERIKSTED.
July 23rd Per schr. Atlantic for St. Tho
mas.—Revd. Romig, Maria L. Canalis &
3 children, Adella Thomas, Jane Petersen,
Louisa Dahlton, Sarah Ventor, Catherine
Jones, Joseph Nathaniel, Steven Bastian,
The Danish barque Malvina, Capt.
Bartram, arrived on Sunday from St.
Thomas.
. e
TELEGRAMS.
ST. CROIX STATION.
New York, July 21st.—DParis despatches
state that General Boulanger’s wound has
healed.
It is reported that M. Duclere, the ex
Premier, is dying.
‘The Clergy gave President Carnot a
hearty welcome at Grenoble. Bishop
Fava, in the course of his remarks, express
ed respect for the authority of the Presi
dent. President Carnot replied that the
Government was pursuing a policy of con
ciliation.
Mr. Conybeare, Member for the Western
Division of Cornwall, has written to the
“Star” a severe letter criticizing the par
tiality of the Speaker in applying the
closure. In the House of Commons Lord
Randolph Churchill interrogated M.
Conybeare respecting the authenticity of
the letter. Mr. Conybeare acknowledged
that he was the author of it, and Lord
Raudolph then wmoved his suspension for
the rest of the session for a gross libel on
the Speaker aund an msult to the House.
Mr. Gladstone supported the motion, de
claring the letter intemperate and a
breach of privilege. Afler some discussion,
Mr. Conybeare was suspended for one
month by 245 to 168.
It is stated that the Atlantic Cable
Companies have decided to double their
tarift.
The Committee of the House of Com
mous has recommended perpetual Sunday
closing in Lreland,
Dr. Ridley, the physician of Tullamore
Jail, committed suicide while attending the
Maudeville inquest. The incident has con
firmed the popular belief as to the mal
treatment of Mr. Mandeville during his
imprisonment.
Plymouth, July 21st.—The Royal Mail
Steamer “Don” from the West [ndies ar
rived bere at nine p. m.
New York, July 23rd.—The death is
announced of M. Duclere, ex Premier of
the French Ministry.
London despatches state that an Autumn
session of Parliament is now certain.
It is believed that the Judges to be
appointed for the Parnpell Inquiry will be
B OB ©2 & A YA4 8
the Rt. Hon. Sir Charles Bowen, Lord
Justice of Appeals; the Hon. Sir Alfred
Wills of the Queen’s Bench; and the Rt.
Hon. Lord Young of the Scotch Court.
Mr. Morley, in the course of a speech at
Morpeth, stated that from trustworthy
information he had received, he belicved
the Parnell Inquiry wounld last a full year
under the proposed Bill. Even, he said,
if some National Leaguers should be
proved guilty of violence, it was still a
serious matter that it should be necessary
to form such a society in order to secure
justice in Ireland.
As a result of the Kaiser's visit to St.
Petersburg, there is a flood of ruamors of
alliances to force France to disarm.
The North German Gazette contains an
article denouucing the dangers of Panslav
ism, which has checked operations on the
Bourse. The article being interpreted as an
indication that Prince Bismarck’s poliey is
unchanged, notwithstauding the inter
change of Russian courtesies.
Paris news states that General Boulan
ger has been defeated in his candidature
for Ordeche.
The Budget Committee has decided to
support the Public Worship estimates.
President Carnot. in a speech at Vizille,
said the people need have no fear for
their rights, and that the Government had
no fear of an attack, as the country had
an able defence, the army being a nation
in itself,

Barbados News,
It is rather an unusnal thing for the
month of July to find estates with the
reaping of their sugar crops unfinished,
At this time this year, however, there are
yet some thousands of tons of sugar to be
manufactured. Since our last Summary
very many estates in the tlats have been
fortunate enouzh to close up altogether.
On the highlands of Christ Church, St.
George, St. John, St. Joseph, St. Thomas,
and St. Andrew, there is yet much sagar
to be reaped. The weather for the past
fortnight has been of a varied characier,
—good winds, but rather showery, the
former favouring the reapers, the latter,
planting operations, The sugar which is
being made continues to bs of good qua
lity, and we are glad to note an upward
tendency in price, both at home and
abroad. The quotations in our local mar
ket to-day are, sugar, $2 35 per 100 lbs,,
and molasses, 15 per gallon and $4 for
puncheon. But the planters refuse to sell
sugar at present rates, hoping at least to
realize $2 50 per 100 Ibs., which weo hope
they may obtain. There is a large quan
tity of sugar in the varions stores in
Bridgetown, as well as on the stanchions
in the country, awaiting botroms for ship
ment. Messrs W, P, Leacock & Co., arve
now loading two Steamers for the United
States, which they hope to dispatch to
morrow. The quantity of produce ship
ped to date has been 33,539 hhids. of sugar,
and 29,473 puns. of molasses, against
43,083 hhds. of sagar, and 32,115 puns,
of molasses at the correspouding period
of last year. Owing to the backward
state of the sugar crop, the planting of
provisions is not so forward throughout
the Island, as it usually is at this time
of the year; althongh, as we stated in our
last Summary, a large amount of root
and cereal crops had been planted in cer
tain parts of the island. The planting of
yams, particularly, is very backward ;
and there is a disposition on the part of
those who control the cultivation of large
properties, to curtail the usual breadth of
potatoes planted at this time of the year.
So far as we have been able to make our
selves conversant with this matter, it
seems to us that this disinclination is
founded on a mistaken policy. 1t is as
serted by some whose experience ought
to be a guarantee for their correctness
that, cheap provisions interfere with the
ready supply of labour ;—the contention,
as we understand jt, being that an abun
dant supply of native provisions encour
ages an abnormal quantity of hawkers,
who are thus taken away from their legiti
mate work in the fields. Sarely, this
seems a reason without a reason, aud is a
contention which will not bear examina
tion. In these days of cheap production
everywhere, we can only hold our owan
by producing cheaply also. The labor
ing classes must eat. And, clearly, they
can live on a swaller wage when the
means of existence are cheap, than other
wise. The planting of provisions by the
estates, whilst adding to the remunera
tive power of those estates, must also
tend to the cheapening of the cost of liv
ing, and of the cost of labour It i 8 in
deed a matter for much regret, and of
some anxiety, that there are sigus of diffi
culty attendant upon our labour
supvly, which is quite new to us. But,
it is scarcely wise to attempt to meet this
difficulty in a defiant spirit, or to try to
solve it by a short-sighted policy. It is
a question which well deserves the best
consideration of the Agricultural Society,
and which ought not to be left to be grap
pled with until it has assumed uunmana
geable dimensions.—Barbados Agricultural
Reporter.

Demerara Items,
(from the Argosy.)
The Planters’ Association and colouists:
in general (saving the Portuguese section
ot the inhabitants)are probably not aware
that in the O Portuguez of Saturday last,—
s weekly newspaper published in George
town, there was a most violent and vicious
attack upon the coolie system' of this co
lony, in which the condition of the East
India immigrant was described in lan
guage that might well create horritied
astonishment in the winds of those who
take it to be true. It may be said that
the best way to treat viralence of tius
kind is with silent contempt; but the
paper in question, althouch not read by
the bulk ot English colonists, finds its way
to other countries, where its seutiments
may gather fictitious imporrance by trans
ference into more important newspapers,
and thereatter be translated into English
and published as the opinion of a journgl
printed in the home of the coolie immigrant.
I'or this reason we do not hesitate to call
attention to the article and to give our
readers a few specimens ot its contents.
The writer begins by reference to- the re
cent dispute over alleged short-prices
on a few East Coast estates, and then
launches forth :—¢The coolie duaring the
“time of his indenture is a veritable slave,
“many times more unhappy than the
“Brazilian slaves in whose favour free
“England has groaned so much. Poor
“reprobates of civilisation. sad waifs of
“fortune. No one defends them either in
“the press orin the legislature. The law
“which rules them is the absolute will of
“the manager, the caprice of the overseer ;
“their wages are what they choose to pay,
“and for the greater part afford only
“dismal bare subsistence ; living. in infec
“ted ranges, without conveniences ;. with
“out suflicient noarvishment; and when
“they must go to the estate’'s hospital, so
“neglected there, that they only mect with
“relief in death,” Before the Sagar-plan
ters, Doctors, Legislators and Officials
read these extracts, they must have been
inignorance of the grossness of their iniqui
ty towards the immigrants; amd, now,
they way find it very difticult to recog
nise themselves in the guise in which they
are here represented. It would be impos
sible to imagine a case in which: actual
facts have been more completely ignored
to make way for statements which are
altogether false and apparently malicious.
The writer of the article, a Portuguese,
long resident in this colony, must be
aware that under our Imwmigration system:
the immigrants are hedged aroand. with
legal protection of a special nature: which.
is not granted to other members of the
commnnity. ‘T'hey are guaranteed. house:
room, doctor’s attendance, hospital accom
modation and a weekly allowaunce of wa
ges far beyond what they ever earned in:
India, and sufficient to enable them to
save considerable sums of mouey, which
have placed them, far and away, the- lead.
ing depositors in the Colony’s Savings
Banks. A more contented body of pre
dial labourers, taking them as a. whole,
are not to be found in any part of the
world; and when, as will occasionally
happen, a few of them here and there
quarrel with the overseers over the rate:
of wages, it is invariably found upon. in--
vestigation, that the dispute bas been de
vised, and the trouble fomented, by some
mischievous rascal, generally a discharged.
driver or some coolie parson. But we
need not go so far from the O Portuguez
to find wnother illustration of the genial
nature of our Immigration laws. At one
time one of the colony’s sources of labour
supply was Madeira, whence came many
hard-working, earnest, penurious peasauts,
who took the colony’s shilling and engag
ed themselves to work on the estates.

X XX. 1
S THERE any simple way of a
looks so she won't be put on
the shelf before her time? I
tm Just this age and a mother and
frandmother, but I feel as young aa
sver I did and don't want to become
fossilized as so many women do at
this age. Ought I to dye my hair, and
iow can I improve my complexion?
I was once thought a beauty and all
f my dear ones are still real proud
f my looks. I want to live as long as
t can and look as well as I can to the
snd of my days. .
"A CONSTANT READER.'
Mother's beauty and health.! Could
ihere be a more endearing subject
for discussion, one nearer to the heart
f every sympathetic human being
who is blessed with a mother? Then
In these days of fine dress and well-
preserved appearances a beauty talk
for women of fifty and sixty Is really
lecessary once In a while.
Let me begin with the hair of the
lderly woman, the mother and young
grandmother who wants to look her
best, Dear woman, or women, drop
the notion of dye, for there is nothing
more aging than hair artificially col
ored. Nature makes provision for the
changes in the face by graying or
blanching the hair, so when the false
brown or black is used every line in
the face is doubly emphasized. One
of the reasons for this is that hair dye
makes a uniform color and the natural
eoloring of the hair is made up of
many shades. So dyed hair frames
the face like a cap, flinging the in
formation in the teeth of all that it is
flyed In fact, it is impossible to hide
the subterfuge even in the case of
very young women.
Gray hair and white hair 'are both
fashionable now, as woman herself
bas learned the folly and ugliness of
hair dyes. But, of course, both gray
and white hair need to be very ten
derly treated ,to look well; in other
words, not to add to the Impression of
Bge.
A stylish coiffure, becoming to the
face, with pins matching the color of
the hair, is always rejuvenating to ap
pearances, and as false pieces are
worn by even the oldest ladies hair
combings should be kept for these. If
there are no combings for the false
pieces, and the head needs more hair,
always match the natural grayness as
much as Is possible in the bought
piece. For silver-errnv locks there are
celluloid pins and other ornaments ex
actly In the same color; there are also
white ones properly shaded for white
hair.
The condition of the complexion has
a great deal to do with general health,
but at any time much benefit may be
gained by sour milk washes. Take e
pint of buttermilk, or sweet milk turn
ed sour, and put a tablespoonful 01
peroxide In it, applying the wash to
the face with a small, clean sponge
after the soap and water bath. Leav
the milk on the face for'half an houi
and then wash it off with tepid water.
This wash is bleaching to the skin
and it is often used for the removal ol
the brown discolorations commonlj
called "moth patches which appear bo
often with middle age on the face,
throat and hands. But one application
of the milk and peroxide is not enough
to bleach the skin, and there is no telf
lng whether the moth patches woui
entirely disappear with a number oí
them. With any beauty formula oni
can only say that in the majority ol
cases the means advised have brought
good results.
Much stress is laid on a light died
of carefully chosen foods. No rul
can be laid down for general follow1
lng, as constitutions differ, but every
body tells of the usefulness of milt
with middle age and old age, and o)
the great need to cut down quantitlei .
of food when the digestion has begui
to impair, as it always does. Milk
cream, eggs, cream cheese, fresl
fruits, cereals and boiled fowl an
boiled fish are everywhere spoken 01
as constituting some of the right die
for the middle-aged and old. The milk
sour and sweet, holds the lactic acid
needed to fight the army of destroy
lng germs which invade the intestines
and by boiling the meats they an
made more digestible and less harm
ful. Meat broths are not bad whei
taken to tone up the stomach befor
other eating, but as they are not nour
isbing they cannot possibly take thv
place of other foods. Soups of al
sorts are considered hard on the kid
neys.
So mother must go back a little nov
to the food of her childhood days i
she wishes to keep her health an
Icoks She must be much in the or-ei
air, sleep well, but not too much, drirl
an adequate quantity of pure wote--take
her warm baths, look afT- h
d!gestion and, above all, see to he
heart's Joy and peace.
She must beware of the "tired" m"
ment, stopping everything in time t
avoid it
She must try and dress with friati
and cultivate interest in all passin;
events.
KATHERINE MORTON.
BELIEFS OF THE MOROCCANS
Curious Ideas of the Arabs Regarding
the Earth, Sleep and Other
Matters.
The Arabs of Morocco believe thai
the earth Is balanced on the horns of
a bull, which itself resta on a fish on
the surface of the sea, the sea being
borne up by the Divine Power. When
the bull is tired he changes the earth
from one horn to the other, with the
result that there is an earthquake!
During sleep the soul leaves the
body and visits other souls. What we
see and hear whilst dreaming are the
things which the soul sees and the
conversations in which it takes part
during its peregrinations. Conse
quently, we ought never to awaken a
sleeper rudely, for if he happens to be
dreaming at that moment his soul
may not have time to return to the
body, and in consequence the man will
die.
Moroccan wizards contend that ghosts
can only be devils, never the souls of
dead people. They are human in ap
pearance, with the exception of their
eyes, which are directed downwards,
and their feet, which are forked or
shaped like the hoof of a camel. These
ghosts speak, but they are Intangible.
They are of all colors yellow, red,
green, white and black and their prin
cipal occupation consists in playing
tricks on human beings.
At Marrakech there is a house in
which the owners placed a bath; but
they were unaware that the property
belonged to the devils. A young wo
man came to have a bath, but no
sooner had she stepped into the water
than she disappeared. Hearing ' her
cries for help", the neighbors rushed in,
whereupon she appeared on the surf
ace of the water. As soon as the
people tried to seize her she disap
peared, and so on for some time.
There was nothing to be done but to
close the house, which thus fell into
the hands of the talehs, who had
spread this story among the credulous
population of the town for their own
end. Wide World Magazine. '
The Cat of St Paul's.
St. Paul's chapel and churchyard, at
Broadway and Fulton streets, Mew
York, have a variety of utilities. Be
sides being a show place of historic
interest to the stranger, they give rest
and soothing at the noon hour to many
a weary worker in the big buildings
which surround them. Not the least
of their usefulness, however, in the
opinion of a certain majestic Thomas
cat, is that they afford a place wherein
he may disport himself. He is an or
namental being of rich, golden hue,
and he is by no means unaware of his
beauty. With stately grace he stalks
about the premises, as one who is re
sponsible for their proper upkeep, and
gazes haughtily at mere human intru
ders. When satisfied they are suffi
ciently cowed to attempt no damage
to his estate he mounts a timeworn
sarcophagus and curls himself up for
his afternoon nap. His yellow coat,
snuggled in a nest of winding ivy.
fairly illumines the greenery and
against the memorial slabs he presents
the combination of gray and gold of
which French couturieres are so fond.
The verger says Thomas belongs to
some tenant in one of the big office
buildings, but he is a regular habitue
of the churchyard.
Our Smallest Library,
A curious little building is situated
at Pinebluff, North Carolina, and is be
lieved to be the smallest public lib
rary in the world. Pinebluff is only
a small place, with a population of
fewer than one hundred in summer,
and when the ladies of the village de
cided that they ought to have a public
library they found themselves with
out a building suitable for the pur
pose. Finally, a gentleman offered
them a band-stand which he had built
in the hope that Pinebluff would soon
become a city, and the offer was ac
cepted with thanks. By giving enter
tainments and, so on, the ladies were
able to raise funds to purchase materi
als to fill in the openings of the band
stand, and generally fit it up Into a
Bnug little building. Over 500 books
have been given and the tiny library,
of which Pinebluff Is very proud, is
now- in full working order. From the
June Wide World.
Looking for a Grievance.
There is nothing quite so easy to
find as a grievance. It can be made
to order in a second's time, no mate
rial other than the imagination being
necessary.
By being on the watch for Insults
and injuries one has little time for the
sweet enjoyments of life.
Plenty of work is the best remedy
for sensitive individuals work and
self-control determination and will.
Half of, one's griefs are only imagin
ary. Have you ever wisnea you mignt
have returned to you the precious
hours spent in suffering over noth
ing? We all need work. We all need rest.
But above all we need the proper light
of understanding that keeps us clear
from the pits of argument, the swamps
of morbidness, the stumbling blocks of
worry and grief.

E USED to appear at the bung
alow at nine o'clock each
morning, his cocoanut-oiled
skin shining like burnished
metal, eyes aglow with readi-
less, hair done up behind like a worn-
w's. He was a young coolie, lithe
ind strong. "Master ready?" "Yes,
fack." "Sir, no. John. 'Jack' all same
Yakka' (imp)." "Yes, John." "Sir,
;hank you. Mrster very good mas-
xr."
Out we go on the long white road he
neen sea and jungle, the flexible
spine of my human steed bending with
jach giant stride, and a myriad silver
3d specks of damp starting out on his
iusky skin. The stately palms are still
is hothouse vines, the sea is a sheet
3f glass, sun-emblazoned, a menace to
:he eye. I open out my big white um-
jrella, and my coolie at once adjusts
lis swinging step to the changed, bal
ince of the little man cart. Others
ickshas. their hoods up, are rolling
in ahead; we come up In their rear,
ind other naked-footed runners pad
jp behind us and join the human
stream. John's back is soon a score
if running rills; but except sometimes
:o lift a hand from his shafts to wipe
lis dripping face with the fringe of
his head cloth, he does not heed.
Suddenly the leader of the long pro
fession pulls up to brush his strag
gling hair from his eyes, and we ar
.n danger of a series of collisions
But the alert following swerves rapid-
.y, and in a second we axe a newly led
xoop, and the delayed rickasha man,
inless he make special efforts, which
may provoke rivalrous sprinting, must
lang on to our rear.
Through Crowded Streets.
Now we come to the Pettah, and my
oolie breaks away from the main body
ind darts into that strange human and
mimal medley which throngs the na
;ive business quarter. 'Mahata! Ma
lata!" (Master! master') he cries,
as if to say, "Give place ihere!" "Ma
hata! Mahata!" answering voices cry,
and some of the lower caste coolies.
almost naked, scurry from before us,
endangering the baskets of freshly
gathered hill-grown tea which they bal
ance on their heads. But the bullock
irivers are of a different tempera
ment. Their guttural cries as they sit
Dn the front of their tented carts and
poke with naked foot and little sticK
and twist the tails of their giant horn
ed, lumbering beasts, almost drown
my coolie's voice. "Hyak! Hyak!"' they
rumble loudly; but the wayward bul
locks scarcely respond, and from time
to time the street is completely block
ed. Portly native merchants, tall Af
ghan money lenders and sellers of
betelnut and other Indian spices
thread their way through the crowd.
and sometimes my vehicle's shafts
are seized by an insignificant Sinhal
ese policeman, and John is forcibly
shouldered through a widening open
ing. Presently he is prancing un
daunted again through the outskirts of
the locality and into the town, till,
with his breast heaving like the sides
of a blown animal, he sets me down
at my office doorway.
Indefatigable John! He could run
for an hour beneath a zenith sun with
out changing his stride. Sometimes
during the ascent of a hill I might tell
him not to hurry. Always he replies:
'Sir, yes. I liking quickly run." "Every
3ay quick, soon die," I would answer,
for it is said that the average Indian
icksha coolie either runs himself to
death or retires in ten years. "Sir,
ao ; running quickly, making plenty
jioney; byemby buying one, two, three
jocoanut tree, marry one girl." "So?"
"Yes sir." "You tninking yon strong,
all same bullock?" I asked. "Master,
10 ; bullock no good, he lazy." "But
only bad white man making coolie run
too fast "Yes sir. Plenty bud whito
man, sir." And he would spring alert
ly ahead and set me down at my des
tlnatio, and, salaaming, retire to the
H
nearest municipal ricksha stand or
shady spot to rest ami chew betelnut,
curling himself up on his shafts un
til some passing pedestrian called him
or I might be ready for him again.
. Asks for Holiday.
One evening, several days ajter I
had freed him for two successive
mornings from his obligation of tak
ing me to business, without deducting
anything from his week's salary, so
that he could journey to a distant
shrine and make his annual salaams
to the Buddha, he asked for a further
holiday to attend a Mohammetan fes
tival. "But you are a Buddhist," I
said. "Sir, please; Mahomet also very
big Sinhalee feller, sir." "But you no
give him salaam?" "No, sir. Other
man give. I take him so can give. He
one very big doctor, sir." John open
ed wide his arms to indicate a man of
immense proportions. "Other coolies
afraid, if taking, he breaking ricksha,
also making coolie very tired. He ask
ing: 'John, I come your ricksha?' I
telling: "If give one present medicine
when I sick in a hurry,' He say,
Yes.' "
"All right, you may go," I said, and
he trotted away.
When, two days-later, he arrived as
usual, I had a headache, my eyes
swam, my limbs felt as if on the rack.
I told Jim, my bungalow boy, to tell
him I had a fever and he could take
still another holiday. Presently Jim
came into the room and stood by my
bed. "Sir, that John coolie telling very
sorry. He staying in compound all
morning; will throw stone at crow in
tree top." "All right" Jim departed
to announce my approval, and a mo
ment later there was the upproarious
sound of a thousand crows rising in
flight from the palm belt round the
compound; then a shout of laughter,
which died away and left the sultry
air noiseless and still; and within the
doorway Jim reappeared, in his hand
a carrion bird.
"That John coolie kill one crow,
sir."
"Bury It," I said wearily, "deep,
deep, away from the ants and snakes."
"Yes, sir." The boy paused a mo
ment, then:
"That John coolie liking bring one
big ünhalee doctor. His friend, sir.
He give master very good medicine, no
asking money."
"No, no," I said, "bring me more qui
nine."
One on A. F. Potts.
Alfred F. Potts, who has just re
turned from California, enjoys telling
the following story on himself: "One
day I dropped into an architect's of
fice in San Diego to inquire about a
building matter. The architect was
reported out for a few minutes, but a
very charming young woman who was
his assistant engaged me in conversa
tion while I waited. She was very
vivacious and attractive. Finally he
turned on me with a pleasant smile
and said:
"'Mr. Potts, aren't you going to
make San Diego your home?'
" 'I'm afraid not,' I replied. 'I have
so many ties and interests in Indian
apolis, you know.'
" "Oh, you ought to break away
while you can, she said. 'San Diego
is the most wonderful place in trie"
world for old people.' " Indianapolis
News.
Plague of Cancer in Finland.
A recent report from Finland shows
that eight times as many men have
cancer on the lip as women. Cfcnce?
of the stomach is about as common ia
women as In men. The cancer com
mission was appointed by the FinnJslI
Medical society and made a study of
the cancers in the hands of 313 phy
sicians. It Is pointed out that caneen
are less common among the upper
class than in the lower class of Fit
land.
MÚ SPOOKS FOR MUGGINS
Waa Afraid of Nothing He Could Un-
derstand, But Apparition Was
Too Much for Him.
Muggins goes to camp with his folks
every summer on the shore of a large
inland lake, and one of the bulldog's
favorite amusements here is in chas
ing the tiny chipmunks which throng
the woods. He never was known to
capture one, but this does not in the
least dampen his enthusiasm for the
pursuit. It is amusing to watch Mug
gins hustle one of these tiny creatures
up into a big tree and then sit pa
tiently at its foot waiting for his prey
to come down which it quite often
does, but on the opposite side of the
trunk, whence it scurries away to pas
tures new, while poor deluded Mug
gins, all unaware of its escape, sits
gazing intensely upward in wide-eyed
anticipation of his quarry's reappear
ance. Muggins despises water, but one day
in his eager rush for a chipmunk he
was over the bank before he knew it
and landed kerflop on his back with
such force as to send him way below
the surface. Disgust spoke from every
feature of his expressive countenance
as he hastily scrambled ashore.
Once as Muggins' master stood in
the boathouse on the lake shore and
saw his favorite trotting toward him,
a mischievous thought came to him
and he prepared to surprise the inno
cent bulldog. Hastily cutting two
round holes for his eyes in a big paper
flour ba;, which lay at hand, he pulled
it down over his head and 'shoulders
and doubled over so as to look alto
geter unnatural. Just as Muggins en
tered the door he sprang forward with
a dismal "boo!" and the dog, who
fears nothing on the face of the earth
which he can understand, jumped
backward, rending the air with a series
of distracted barks, then tore wildly
away on a race for his life, without
even a backward glance at the awful
apparition. He did not stop at camp,
but fled into a field far above, where
he installed himself in the open and
continued his excited barking for over
three hours, when he was finally
coaxed back to quarters by his re
pentant tormentor.
Therr They Hit Up the Sirup.
"While on a western tour in connec
tion with certain investigations of the
committee on Indian affairs, Senator
Carroll E. Page of Vermont happened
to get in conversation with a man at
Ashland, Wis.
"From Vermont?" chuckled the
man. Why, I was born at Swanton,
Vt."
"So was I," said the senator.
"My father ran a store on Merch
ant's row in Swanton."
"So did mine," replied the Vermont
senator. v
"My father dealt in hides and wool."
"So did mine," echoed the senator.
"My father was a member of the
firm of Page, Sanborn & Co."
"So was mine," cried Senator Page,
with a twinkle in his eye. "Shake!
And they "shook" heartily.
In this unexpected way the sons of
the old Vermont partners had met
after 40 years, and tradition has it
that they went off quietly arm in arm
for a feast of maple sirup, in memory
of childhood days at Swanton In the
old Green Mountain state. Joe Chap
pie's Newsletter.
The Burnt Child.
A rural clergyman missed one of
his parishioners several successive
Sundays from his place in church and
when he met the absentee one day he
said:
"Well, William, I haven't seen you
at church for some time."
"No, air. I have reasons for stayin
away."
"Oh, you have! And what may your
reasons be? I should like to explain
them away, if possible."
"Well, sir, I doubt you'll manage
that. They ' are very decided objec
tions. The first is that I don't be
lieve in beln' where one does all the
speakin'; the second Is that I don't be
lieve in so much singin' as we get in
your church; the third, and last, and
most important reason of all is that
it was In your church that I got my
wife!"
Live Your Life.
We sometimes wonder of what use
we are, and why we are put on earth.
One day is added to another and we
seem to be no further advanced on the
pathway of our lives. As Mark Twain
would have put it, we do not appear
to be gaining on the scenery. Yet
there is room In the world, and need
in the world, for each and every one
of us, and, therefore, we must keep on
going to the end. Emerson has a
poem in which the squirrel talks to
the mountain and says::, "If I cannot
carry forests on my back neither can
you crack a nut." You can do some
thing nobody else can do, namely.
live your life. You have the chance,
if you will only take it, and I have
mine. If we can do nothing else we
can at least be some one's friend, and
there is nothing that the world more
keenly wants and sadly needs. La
dies' Home Journal.
Very Fond of Smith.
"You are fond of Smith, aren't you?"
"Very."
"He hasn't much character?"
"No."
"His manners are bearish."
"Yes."
"He is dull."
"True."
"Then what do you find so attract
ive in him?"
"He has never asked me for a
thine." Newark News.
ULTIMATE ROAD TO SUCCESS
Dlever Poet, With Beautiful Style and
Nice Touch, Makes Fortune Out
of Baseball Dope.
"What's become of Kimberley?'
"Kimberley? I don't remember any,
such chap."
"Oh, yes you do. He used to be re
garded as the ablest literary man In
this town. Wrote essays, poems, his
torical monographs and that sort of
thing."
"Oh, yes, yes. I remember him
Algernon Kimberley. Why, he's rich
and famous now. The fact that you
didn't know all about him was what
made me suppose you must be refer
ring to some other Kimberley. Yes,
Klmberley's struck It rich."
"Indeed? I'm mightly glad to hear
that. A very clever chap beautiful
style, nice touch and all that sort of
thing. I'm surprised that I have not
heard of his success before. But I
always had an idea that his greatness
would be recognized. What line has
he succeeded in? I hope it's poetry.
I think he has the true poetic spirit,
and his expression is rather original.
We need a great poet just at this
time."
"Poetry? I should say not. After
he had nearly starved writing poetry
and essays and historical monographs
he went to writing baseball dope and
syndicating it. It went great, and I
understand he's making over $500 a
week."

It had been understood by the Dixon i
and the Taylor families, and by most
of the people for Ave miles around,
for that matter, that Tom Dixon and
Bessie Taylor became engaged as they
were riding homo together from the
county fair In October. The exact
date and exact circumstances of such
things are always of vital Interest to
an agricultural community. . .
It was a match that pleased .both
families, as Tom was a fine young far
mer, and Bessie a smart girl of nine
teen, who could have had the pick. of
half a dozen.
The marriage was not to take place
for a year, and the course of true love
ran smooth until December. Then
Bessie Taylor suddenly exercised the
prerogative of her sex.
Tom was too sure of her, and he
must be made to understand that his
bird was not yet caged.
There was to be a spelling bee at
the red school house. He and Bessie
would go, of course.' As both of them
were accounted among the best spell
ers in the community, It was, probable
that they might be asked to choose
aides. ' . "
It was unfortunate far-Tom that he
forgot to say that he would drive up
to Taylor's at a certain hour- for Bes
sie. He meant to, and he bad no
doubt that she . would be ready,, but
the matter slipped his mind, and when
the evening finally arrived, ho got out
of his cutter at the doer,, only to be
told that Bessie had been gone' half
an hour.
More than that, she had gone "with
a windmill man who was canvassing
the neighborhood end stopping at her
father's house.
Tom Dixon was stunned. Here was
coquetry, treachery, deceit. It dldnt
occur to him that Bessie felt piqued
over his neglect and wanted to "get
even" in the only way open to her.
Neither could he know that she had
told the windmill man that' her old
beau would probably escort her borne.
He gasped he muttered he swore.
Then he got into his cutter and put
the whip to his horse and sent the ani
mal over two miles of smooth road on
dead run
His first Idea was to kill that wind
mill roan oa sight, his second was to
kill the pair of deceivers, his third
was to blow his own brains out and
die the death of a martyr.. Then he
happened to get a fourth idea, and he
adopted it and stuck, to it
He entered the schoolhouse with his
Jaw set and 'a firm resolve to make a

Eccentricities of Genius.
One of the first things she did as
soon as the success of her book be
came the talk of Paris was to fly from
the city into a hidden retreat, and
no communication from the outside
world was tolerated by her not even
her letters were forwarded." The
lady with this remarkable genius for
shyness is Mme. Marcelle Tinayre,
author of "La Malson de Peche." She
gave some Interesting advice at
later time to another lady who "be
came the talk of Paris." This was
Mdlle. Thouret, who tried to shoot
M. Marcel Prevost. M. Prevost had
made free use of Mdlle. Thouret's pri
vate letters In one of his romances.
"Why shoot at him, my dear?" wrote
Mme. Tinayre. "You did not hit him
and Paris now laughs at you. Now
If you had printed his letters Paris
would have laughed at him."

"This," recently remarked Joseph
Chamberlain, England's colonial secre
tary, as he placed a finger on a map
showing Britain's possessions in the
West Indies, "Is 'the paradise of the
Hindoo coolie.
"And there, too," added one of his
hearers, "those same orientals have
created an atmosphere that fairly
reeks with romance."
The American or European who has
pent sufficient time In Jamaica or
Trinidad to become acquainted with
the ways of the thousands ot Hindoo
laborers on the plantations and tbe
coolie tradesmen In the towns inva
riably makes the same assertions. To
prove the first he usually quotes the
case of Ram Sal and as evidence of
the latter he tells the story of a
rajah's son who turned' coolie for
love's sake.
Thirty years ago a Hindoo of low
caste Ram Sal by name, living in a
plateau town of India, found himself
Inextricably in debt to a bunnla a
usurious money lender. He bad been
bound In his boyhood for the debt of
his grandfather and after working for
nearly twenty years to clear it ho
learned to his distress that Instead of
becoming smaller the obligation had
steadily Increased.
Ram Sal saw starvation staring htm'
self and his family in the face, and
so, when a European, coming to the
village, told of a place across the
"black water" where a Hindoo could
earn as much as 25 cents a day and
after binding himself to work for eight
years on a plantation would be freed
of both his contract and his debt, Ram
Sal straightway prepared to follow the

Tbe ship that carried Ram Sal and

ened his ambition and he vowed then

To that goal, during the remaining

.his present purpose, the establish
ment of himself as a silversmith In
the Hindoo quarter of Port of Spain
In which business he prospered rap
idly.

Ram Sal was now fairly started on

Thus Ram Sal prospered until two,

That Is why Mr. Chamberlain, who

The eldest sou and heir of a rapah
of one of the small states of Rajpu-
tana fell so violently in love with a
ueautiful maiden he had seen on tbe
streets of his father's capital that he
laid aside all thoughts of his own high

and proceeded to woo her. Every

During the weeHs that energetic

He had worked as a common field
laborer for five years before his story
accidentally became known. Over a
certain number of coolies another
coolie Is usually placed as overseer
One day the rajah's son and his over
seer disagreed over a piece of work.
and the latter was ou . the point ot
using a whip on the former when the
plantation's superintendent, a young
Scotchman, interfered.
The gratitude of the laborer was
unbounded. For once he lost his
oriental stoicism and tears came into
his eyes, as he said:
"Master, you have saved me from
a great disgrace."
The superintendents curiosity was
aroused, and although the Hindoo en
deavored to slink away he was pressed
so hard that he finally explained his
remark. . .
"You have saved me from a great
disgrace," he said. "I, a rajah's son,
to be struck by a Hindoo of low
caste!"
Then the whole story, which the
Scotchman verified later on, came out.
The hero of it Is still employed on an
Interior plantation Jn Jamaica. "
